When small, they run across rivers with such speed that the water has no time to sink them; but when they are large, they cross rivers by walking along the bottom underwater, because they are heavy and do not know how to swim. They are raised in great numbers in the kingdom of Tierra Firme and along all the coasts of New Spain; and during Lent many are sold in the towns there. While alive, with their necks raised, they strike terror into whoever looks upon them.

They are caught in the following manner: they ordinarily sleep in trees, and in the mornings they are stiff and numb, so that when the trees are shaken, they fall to the ground so stupefied that they can barely move, and at that point they are taken by hand. They are also commonly hunted in the same trees with snares, and in the rivers as well, because they cannot bite while they are in the water; outside of it, however, they can, if the catcher is not careful. The iguana produces in its head a stone [bezoar-like calculus] that is very efficacious for dissolving [kidney and bladder] stones.

entities_mentioned.name: iguana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iguana iguana (green iguana) or related species of the family Iguanidae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes the term derives from the language of Hispaniola. He classifies it as a 'pescado' (fish) for dietary/ecclesiastical purposes, allowing its consumption during Lent. He describes its physical characteristics, behavior in captivity, habitat, and culinary use. He also describes small young running across water surfaces — consistent with the basilisk lizard behavior sometimes attributed to iguanas in colonial sources, though possibly confused here. The 'stone from the head' (piedra de la cabeza) is described as medicinally useful for dissolving kidney and bladder stones [calculi]; this likely refers to a cranial or jaw concretion used as a folk remedy, analogous to bezoar stones.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra de la iguana (stone of the iguana)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown biological concretion; possibly a cranial or jaw bone fragment used as a folk remedy
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing in the iguana's head and used medicinally to break up [dissolve] kidney and bladder stones. This is a lapidary/bezoar tradition common in colonial natural history. Condition treated: urolithiasis (kidney and bladder stones). Preparation method: not described; presumably administered internally in powdered or whole form.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Pescado' (lit. 'fish') is used in the ecclesiastical-dietary sense: iguanas were classified as permissible Lenten food in colonial Spanish America, a classification that drove significant commercial trade during Lent as noted by Cobo. 2. 'Palmo' is a unit of measure approximately 20–21 cm (roughly 8 inches); translated as 'hand-span' for accessibility. 3. The claim that young iguanas run across water surfaces is zoologically plausible for the basilisk lizard (Basiliscus spp.) but less so for Iguana iguana; Cobo may be conflating species or reporting local folklore. 4. OCR errors corrected silently include: 'Mtttraleza' → 'naturaleza'; 'mm fiero' → 'más fiero'; 'rad parece' → 'cual parece'; 'tfeae' → 'tiene'; 'Im uñas' → 'las uñas'; 'w, la cola' → 'yor, la cola'; 'Mfis' → 'más'; 'Asemeja' → 'asemeja'; 'i) asan' → 'pasan'; 'j>ie' → 'pie'; 'Cogénlas' → 'Cógenlas'; 'río»' → 'ríos'; 'agiia' → 'agua'. 5. The segment is primarily zoological/natural history in nature rather than materia medica, though the medicinal use of the iguana head-stone is noteworthy.

results.title_inferred: On Sardines and Anchovies of the South Sea Coast
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIII
De las sardinas y anchovetas

En todas las costas de la mar del Sur desta América Austral se crían sardinas en gran abundancia, señaladamente en la del arzobispado de Lima, obispado de Arequipa y reino de Chile; que parece quiso Nuestro Señor proveer a los moradores destas provincias marítimas del Perú de remedio contra la esterilidad de sus tierras. Porque, como antiguamente era necesario en algunas costas de las diócesis de Lima y Arequipa echar sardinas con las semillas que sembraban, para que diesen fruto copioso, fuera muy costoso y difícil este beneficio, si no se hallaran tan a mano y en tanta abundancia las sardinas. Las cuales se crían en tanta cantidad en las costas dichas, que la mar suele echar a tierra gran suma déllas, con que los indios estercolaban y fertilizaban las tierras de labor marítimas, y tenían abundancia de pescado con que mantenerse.

La costa de Chile hace ventaja a las otras desta mar del Sur en esta abundancia de sardinas, las cuales se aderezam en aquel reino, especialmente en la isla de Chiloé, como los arenques de España; de donde se traen a esta ciudad de Lima y se estiman por cosa regalada; si bien no son tan grandes como las sardinas arenques de Europa.

No es menor la copia que hay de anchovetas en las mismas costas; las cuales también algunas veces, por su multitud, varan en tierra y se quedan en seco, particularmente cuando son perseguidas por peces grandes; y cuando así dan en tierra, suele quedar un gran camellón déllas a lo largo de la playa, como yo lo vi una vez, que no fue posible agotarlas. Suelen venir por esta costa del Perú tan espesos cardúmenes de anchovetas, que, navegando yo de Lima a Trujillo el año de 1627, nos cercó el navio uno tan grande, que parecía una mancha negra el agua, y por estar a la sazón en calma, las cogía la gente del navio a canastos, con no más trabajo que meter los canastos de canto en la mar y sacarlos llenos de anchovetas. En esta ciudad de Lima y en su comarca se gasta todo el año gran cantidad de anchovetas frescas, y se tiene por pescado regalado y de muy buen sabor; fuera de que es gran socorro para la gente pobre, porque con un real de anchovetas cena toda la gente de una casa, aunque sean diez o doce personas.

Cuando veo tan grande inmensidad de anchovetas en esta mar del Sur, vengo a sentir, que así como crió Dios la hierba en los campos para pasto de animales terrestres, así también crió las anchovetas en la mar para sustento de los acuátiles; porque todo género de pescado mayor y menor, con otra infinidad de aves marinas, se mantienen déllas; y los pescadores no ponen de ordinario en los anzuelos otra carnada o cebo que de anchovetas, para pescar todo género de peces. También se secan muchas al sol y se llevan a varias partes.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIII
On Sardines and Anchovies

Along all the coasts of the South Sea [Mar del Sur] of this Southern America, sardines breed in great abundance, most notably along the coast of the archbishopric of Lima, the bishopric of Arequipa, and the kingdom of Chile. It seems that Our Lord wished to provide the inhabitants of these maritime provinces of Peru with a remedy against the barrenness of their lands. For, since in ancient times it was necessary along certain coasts of the dioceses of Lima and Arequipa to mix sardines in with the seeds they sowed, so that the land might yield a plentiful harvest, this practice would have been very costly and difficult were sardines not so readily at hand and in such great abundance. These fish breed in such quantities along the said coasts that the sea is wont to cast great numbers of them ashore, wherewith the Indians manured and fertilized their coastal farmlands, and had an abundance of fish to sustain themselves.

The coast of Chile surpasses the other coasts of this South Sea in this abundance of sardines, which are cured in that kingdom—especially on the island of Chiloé—in the same manner as herrings in Spain. From there they are brought to this city of Lima and are esteemed as a delicacy, though they are not as large as the herring-sardines [sardinas arenques] of Europe.

No less plentiful are the anchovies [anchovetas] along these same coasts. These likewise sometimes, on account of their great multitude, run aground and are left stranded in the dry, particularly when they are pursued by large fish; and when they beach themselves in this manner, a great ridge [camellón] of them is left along the shore—as I myself once witnessed—so vast that it was impossible to exhaust them. Schools [cardúmenes] of anchovies so dense as to be remarkable are common along this Peruvian coast; indeed, while sailing from Lima to Trujillo in the year 1627, our vessel was surrounded by a school so enormous that the water appeared as a black stain, and since the sea happened to be calm at the time, the ship's crew caught them by the basketful, with no more effort than dipping the baskets edgewise into the sea and drawing them out full of anchovies. In this city of Lima and its surrounding district, great quantities of fresh anchovies are consumed throughout the year and are regarded as a delicacy of very fine flavor; beyond which, they are a great relief to the poor, for with one real's worth of anchovies, an entire household—even of ten or twelve persons—can have its supper.

When I contemplate the immense multitude of anchovies in this South Sea, I come to feel that just as God created grass in the fields for the sustenance of land animals, so too He created anchovies in the sea for the sustenance of aquatic creatures; for every manner of fish, large and small, together with a countless multitude of seabirds, feed upon them. Fishermen likewise habitually use no other bait or lure on their hooks than anchovies, in order to catch all manner of fish. Great numbers of them are also dried in the sun and carried to various places.

entities_mentioned.name: sardinas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: South American pilchard / Sardinops sagax (Pacific sardine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes their use as agricultural fertilizer (mixed with seeds) along the coasts of Lima and Arequipa, as well as their curing in the manner of European herrings on the island of Chiloé.

entities_mentioned.name: anchovetas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peruvian anchovy / Engraulis ringens
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as extraordinarily abundant along the Peruvian coast, used as food for the poor, as fishermen's bait, and dried for transport. Cobo's eyewitness account of the 1627 school near Lima is notable.

entities_mentioned.name: arenques de España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atlantic herring / Clupea harengus
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparative standard for curing method, not as a New World species.

results.translator_notes: The OCR produced several corrupted passages in the opening paragraph ('fcta', 'b del arzolaspado', 'k Arequipa', 'teña', 'tos', 'í^aWaban') which have been silently corrected to their unambiguous originals based on context. The word 'camellón' (literally a raised ridge or furrow between irrigation channels) is used here metaphorically to describe a windrow or bank of stranded fish along the beach; it has been translated as 'ridge' with the original in brackets preserved in the cleaned text. 'Regalado' is a period term meaning choice, prized, or delicate (as in food), rendered here as 'delicacy.' The passage contains no medical or pharmacological content; it is natural-history and economic description.

results.title_inferred: Fish of the South Sea: salmon-trout, guavinas, vermejuelos, barbels, bacallao, corvinas, angel-fish, cuttlefish, bonito, mackerel, sierra, and horse-mackerel
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVII

De los salmonados, guavinas, dihachu, vermejuelos, barbos, bacallao, corvinas, peje-ángelo y jibia

Estas nueve especies de pescados se crían en esta mar del Sur, aunque no son tan comunes como otros. El bacallao es menor que el de España, porque no se halla mayor que de una tercia y es raro el que se mata. El peje-ángelo tiene el cuero muy duro y áspero, del cual usan los carpinteros y escultores para raspar la madera. La jibia de acá no tiene aquella concha que la de España; es de media braza de largo.

CAPITULO XXXVIII

De los bonitos, caballas, sierras y jureles

Estas cuatro especies de pescados parece que tienen entre sí parentesco, porque son de igual calidad en ser manjar grosero y de poca estima, y que no suelen comerlo sino a falta de otro más regalado y sano; y con todo eso son muy sabrosos al gusto, particularmente el bonito, y más si está salpresado, que entonces se parece al atún en el sabor. Crianse copiosamente todos estos pescados en esta mar del Sur, y apenas hay día que no los pesquen en el puerto del Callao; y cuando acude algún cardumen de bonitos, que es muy frecuentemente, son innumerables los que se matan; y suelen venderse tan baratos, que dan dos o tres al real, y cuando más caros valen a real o dos reales cada uno. Es el bonito el mismo pescado que el saltón, de media vara de largo, y pesa de cuatro a seis libras cada uno.

Las caballas son tan comunes en esta costa y puerto del Callao como los bonitos. Suelen venir a menudo grandes cardúmenes, y con la abundancia bajan tanto de precio, que las he visto yo vender en el Callao veinticinco al real. Las caballas y bonitos son pescados tan simples, o, por mejor decir, golosos, que no es menester poner carnada en los anzuelos para prenderlos, más que un trapillo blanco, que al punto pican y caen; y de la caballa hacen los pescadores carnada, para pescar otros peces.

La sierra es muy parecida al bonito, aunque es menor, más delgada y menos comida; tiene unas pintas doradas por en medio; y no se matan tantas sierras como caballas y bonitos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII

On the salmonados [salmon-trout], guavinas, dihachu, vermejuelos [small red fish], barbels, bacallao [cod], corvinas, angel-fish, and cuttlefish

These nine species of fish are found in this South Sea, although they are not as common as others. The bacallao [cod] is smaller than that of Spain, for none is found larger than a third of a vara [approximately 28 cm], and it is rarely caught. The peje-ángelo [angel-fish] has a very tough and rough skin, which carpenters and sculptors use for scraping wood. The cuttlefish here does not have the shell [cuttlebone] that the Spanish one has; it is half a braza [approximately 84 cm] in length.

CHAPTER XXXVIII

On the bonito, mackerel [caballas], sierra, and horse-mackerel [jureles]

These four species of fish appear to be related to one another, for they are of equal quality in being coarse fare and of little esteem, such that people do not usually eat them except for want of something more delicate and wholesome; and yet for all that they are very pleasing to the palate, particularly the bonito, and more so if it has been salt-pressed, for then it resembles tuna in flavor. All of these fish are found in great abundance in this South Sea, and there is scarcely a day when they are not caught in the port of Callao; and when a school of bonito appears—which happens very frequently—the numbers taken are beyond counting. They are commonly sold so cheaply that two or three are given for a real, and at their most expensive they fetch a real or two reales each. The bonito is the same fish as the saltón, half a vara [approximately 42 cm] in length, and each one weighs between four and six pounds.

The mackerel [caballas] are as common on this coast and in the port of Callao as the bonito. Large schools come frequently, and with such abundance their price falls so low that I have myself seen them sold at twenty-five to the real in Callao. The mackerel and bonito are fish so simple—or, better said, so greedy—that there is no need to bait the hooks to catch them; a small scrap of white cloth is enough, and they immediately bite and are taken. Fishermen use the mackerel as bait to catch other fish.

The sierra is very similar to the bonito, except that it is smaller, slimmer, and less eaten; it has golden spots along its middle; and sierra are not taken in as great numbers as mackerel and bonito.

entities_mentioned.name: salmonados
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a salmon-like or salmon-colored fish of the South Pacific; possibly refers to Oncorhynchus or a related trout/salmon species
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic descriptive term meaning 'salmon-like'; exact species unclear in the Pacific context.

entities_mentioned.name: guavinas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Gobiidae (gobies) or Eleotridae; the term 'guavina' in Latin American usage often refers to freshwater/brackish gobies
entities_mentioned.notes: Term used broadly for various small fish in colonial Spanish America.

entities_mentioned.name: dihachu
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a local indigenous name; not identified in standard ichthyological literature. Possible OCR distortion.

entities_mentioned.name: vermejuelos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly small red-colored coastal fish; perhaps a species of Lutjanidae (snappers) or similar
entities_mentioned.notes: Diminutive of 'vermejo' (red); descriptive color name only.

entities_mentioned.name: barbos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barbels; in a Pacific context possibly refers to a barbel-like fish or Mugilidae (mullet); true Barbus not native to South America
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial authors sometimes applied familiar European fish names loosely to morphologically similar New World species.

entities_mentioned.name: bacallao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cod-like fish; possibly Gadus macrocephalus (Pacific cod) or more likely a locally abundant species resembling cod
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes it is smaller than Spanish bacallao (Atlantic cod, Gadus morhua); it is rare along the South American Pacific coast.

entities_mentioned.name: corvinas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cilus gilberti or Cynoscion analis (Peruvian corvina / drum fish, family Sciaenidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Corvina remains a well-known food fish on the Peruvian coast today.

entities_mentioned.name: peje-ángelo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a shark or ray species with rough denticle-covered skin (e.g., dogfish, family Squalidae, or an angel shark, Squatinidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes the skin as very hard and rough, used by craftsmen for sanding wood—consistent with shark skin (shagreen). 'Peje-ángelo' literally means 'angel fish' but in colonial usage often referred to angel sharks (Squatina).

entities_mentioned.name: jibia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuttlefish; likely Sepia or a related cephalopod; Cobo notes it lacks a cuttlebone unlike the European Sepia officinalis, possibly Dosidicus gigas (Humboldt squid) or a local Sepia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's observation that it lacks the internal shell ('concha'/cuttlebone) of the European jibia is notable; this may indicate a squid-like cephalopod rather than a true cuttlefish.

entities_mentioned.name: bonito / saltón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sarda chiliensis (Pacific bonito)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates bonito with saltón; described as half a vara long and 4–6 pounds. Abundant at Callao. Salt-pressed bonito compared to tuna in flavor.

entities_mentioned.name: caballas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scomber japonicus (Pacific chub mackerel) or Trachurus murphyi
entities_mentioned.notes: Extremely abundant and cheap at Callao; used as bait by fishermen. Taken with bare white-cloth lures.

entities_mentioned.name: sierras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thyrsites atun (snoek) or Scomberomorus sierra (Pacific sierra mackerel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as smaller and slimmer than bonito, with golden spots along the middle.

entities_mentioned.name: jureles
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trachurus murphyi (Peruvian jack mackerel / horse mackerel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Jureles are a staple of the Peruvian/Chilean coast to this day.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'baculho' → 'bacallao'; 'xina tercia' → 'una tercia'; 'condia' → 'concha'; 'corvina*tas,,' → 'corvinas'; 'algún' reconstructed from 'algon'; 'baratos' from 'kratos'; 'simples' from 'ámples'; 'sierras' from 'sierras'. The term 'dihachu' may be an indigenous Quechua or other Andean-language fish name and may also contain an OCR error; it is left as found. 'Salpresado' [salt-pressed, i.e., salt-cured] is preserved as a technical food-preparation term. Cobo's price data (2–3 bonitos per real; 25 mackerel per real) is significant economic history and has been rendered faithfully. The use of angel-fish / peje-ángelo skin as an abrasive by craftsmen is an ethnographic detail of interest.

results.title_inferred: On the sharks and shark-like fishes of the Peruvian coast
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVIII
De los cazones

En toda la costa deste reino del Perú se cría gran copia de cazones de todas especies conocidas en España y aún de otras propias de las Indias, y son conocidos por los nombres siguientes: cazón pique; otro se llama cazón negrito; otros canahotas, dentudo, guayas, que llaman en España albariños. A éstos, que son propios cazones, se reducen otros peces por alguna semejanza y parentesco que tienen con ellos, y participan del nombre de cazones, principalmente porque no tienen escama, sino cuero, como son mielga, la cual es un pece que tiene púas en el lomo; pintarroja, que es como cazón mediano algo prieto; el llamado peje-gata, que es de una o dos varas de largo, de hechura de cazón, y tiene muy gran cabeza; el peje-zorra, que es propio de Indias, y tiene dos andanas de dientes, el cuero sutil y dos varas de largo, con mayor cola que cuerpo; el peje-guitarra, y el peje-gallo. Hállase otro peje a manera de cazón de una vara de largo, de gran cuero áspero y mayor cabeza que cuerpo. La picuda tiene dos varas de largo; es pescado que suele comer manzanilla, fruta ponzoñosa. El pece llamado cornuda es también especie de cazón; tiene particular hechura, porque, teniendo el cuerpo y tamaño de cazón, le nace sobre la boca y labio alto una jeta que tiene de largo un palmo y de ancho tres dedos, en forma de hierro de aguijada; y tiene los ojos no en la cabeza, como los demás animales de tierra y agua, sino en la orilla desta jeta, que parece le sirve de orejas. Es pescado éste que no le tienen por de buen mantenimiento comido fresco, y los indios pescadores, por venderlo por cazón, le suelen cortar la jeta en que están los ojos; mas, es fácil de advertir el engaño en reparando que está sin ojos.

El peje-gallo es muy hermoso; tiene de largo de dos a tres palmos, cuero muy delicado y de un color plateado y reluciente; sobre la nariz un pico como de gallo, y debajo dél una cresta, y la boca de particular hechura, con unas aletas grandes sobre el vientre, que le salen de la cabeza, de dos palmos de punta a punta, y otras dos aletas menores abajo del vientre, y una cola que va adelgazando hasta acabar tan delgada en la punta como de ratón, y de una tercia de largo.

Hay cazones de diferente grandeza; los menores y de más regalada comida son de una tercia o media vara de largo; y de aquí van creciendo hasta hallarse algunos de dos varas que parecen tiburones. De todos estos géneros de cazones se matan en el Callao y se venden frescos en Lima; y dellos y de tiburones se hace gran cantidad de tollo en muchos puertos, particularmente en el de Páyta, diócesis de Trujillo, y en el de Huarmey, deste arzobispado de Lima.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVIII
On the Sharks [cazones]

Along the entire coast of this kingdom of Peru there grows a great abundance of sharks [cazones] of all the species known in Spain, and also of others native to the Indies. They are known by the following names: cazón pique; another is called cazón negrito; others are canahotas, dentudo, guayas — which in Spain they call albariños. To these, which are true sharks [cazones], are also reckoned other fish by virtue of some resemblance and kinship they share with them, and they partake of the name of sharks chiefly because they have no scales, but rather a hide [cuero], such as the mielga [spiny dogfish], which is a fish that has spines on its back; the pintarroja [spotted dogfish/rockfish], which is like a medium-sized shark, somewhat dark in color; the one called peje-gata [cat shark], which is one or two varas [yards] in length, shaped like a shark, and has a very large head; the peje-zorra [fox shark / thresher shark], which is native to the Indies, has two rows of teeth, a fine hide, and is two varas in length, with a tail larger than its body; the peje-guitarra [guitar fish]; and the peje-gallo [cockerel fish / chimaera]. There is found yet another fish in the manner of a shark, one vara in length, with a large rough hide and a head bigger than its body. The picuda [barracuda] is two varas in length; it is a fish that is known to eat manzanilla [manchineel], a poisonous fruit. The fish called cornuda [hammerhead shark] is also a species of shark; it has a peculiar form, for, while having the body and size of a shark, growing from above its mouth and upper lip is a snout [jeta] that is a palm [palmo] in length and three fingers in width, in the shape of a goad's iron tip; and it has its eyes not in its head, as do other animals of land and water, but along the edge of this snout, which appears to serve it as ears. This is a fish not considered wholesome food when eaten fresh, and the Indian fishermen, in order to sell it as [ordinary] shark, are accustomed to cut off the snout on which the eyes are set; however, the deceit is easy to detect upon noticing that it lacks eyes.

The peje-gallo [chimaera / rabbit fish] is very handsome; it measures two to three palms in length, has a very delicate hide of a silvery and shining color; above the snout it has a beak like a cockerel's, and beneath it a crest, and its mouth is of a peculiar shape, with large fins on the belly that extend from the head, measuring two palms from tip to tip, and two smaller fins below the belly, and a tail that tapers until it ends as thin at the tip as a mouse's tail, and one third of a vara in length.

Sharks come in different sizes; the smaller ones, which make the most delicate eating, are one third to half a vara in length; from there they increase in size until some are found of two varas that look like true sharks [tiburones]. All these kinds of sharks are caught at Callao and sold fresh in Lima; and from them and from sharks [tiburones] a great quantity of dried salt fish [tollo] is made in many ports, particularly in that of Paita, in the diocese of Trujillo, and in that of Huarmey [Guarmey], in this archdiocese of Lima.

entities_mentioned.name: cazón pique
entities_mentioned.notes: A named local variety of shark; specific species not determinable from description alone.

entities_mentioned.name: cazón negrito
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for its dark coloration; species indeterminate.

entities_mentioned.name: canahotas
entities_mentioned.notes: Local name; possibly a smooth-hound or similar small shark.

entities_mentioned.name: dentudo
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for prominent teeth; species indeterminate.

entities_mentioned.name: guayas / albariños
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates 'guayas' (local Andean name) with 'albariños' used in Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: mielga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Squalus acanthias (spiny dogfish) or related squalid shark
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having spines on the back and no scales; classic description of the spiny dogfish.

entities_mentioned.name: pintarroja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scyliorhinus canicula or related catshark (Scyliorhinidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a medium-sized, somewhat dark shark; pintarroja is the traditional Spanish name for the lesser-spotted dogfish / small-spotted catshark.

entities_mentioned.name: peje-gata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A large catshark or nurse shark-type; possibly Ginglymostoma cirratum or local equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as one to two varas long, shark-shaped, with a very large head.

entities_mentioned.name: peje-zorra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alopias vulpinus (common thresher shark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Two rows of teeth, slender hide, tail longer than body — classic thresher shark description. Noted as native to the Indies.

entities_mentioned.name: peje-guitarra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhinobatidae spp. (guitar fish / guitarfish)
entities_mentioned.notes: Guitar fish; a ray-like elasmobranch common on Pacific coasts.

entities_mentioned.name: peje-gallo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Callorhinchus callorynchus (elephant fish / cock-nosed chimaera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described with a cock-like beak above the snout, crest, silvery delicate skin, large pectoral fins, and a rat-like tapering tail — strongly matches the chimaera Callorhinchus callorynchus found along the Pacific South American coast.

entities_mentioned.name: picuda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sphyraena spp. (barracuda)
entities_mentioned.notes: Noted for eating manzanilla (manchineel fruit), which may explain ciguatera-like toxicity attributed to barracuda in colonial sources.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hippomane mancinella (manchineel tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a poisonous fruit; the manchineel is notoriously toxic. Cobo's claim that the picuda eats it is a folk explanation for fish toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: cornuda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sphyrna spp. (hammerhead shark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Description of a wide, flat rostral extension ('jeta') with eyes at its edges is an unambiguous description of the hammerhead shark. Cobo notes it is not considered good fresh food, and that Indian fishermen would cut off the cephalofoil to pass it off as ordinary shark.

entities_mentioned.name: tollo
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried and/or salted shark flesh; a commodity noted as produced in large quantities at the ports of Paita and Huarmey.

entities_mentioned.name: tiburón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Large shark spp. (generic term)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here to distinguish large sharks from the smaller cazones.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source label 'reino del fm' at the beginning is an OCR error for 'reino del Perú'; corrected silently. 2. 'lodai' is an OCR error for 'todas'; corrected. 3. 'canahotas' appears as a single word but may represent 'cañahotas', another name used in Spain for small sharks. 4. 'paa o dos varas' corrected to 'una o dos varas'. 5. 'Guainhacho' is an OCR error for 'Huarmey' (also spelled Guarmey or Guarme in colonial documents), a port in the archdiocese of Lima; corrected with notation. 6. The footnote marker '(13)' in the original likely refers to a glossary or editorial note on 'tollo' in the modern edition used as source; not reproduced in translation. 7. Cobo's claim that the picuda eats manchineel fruit as an explanation for its toxicity is a period folk-medicine belief and should be read in that context. 8. The description of Indian fishermen removing the hammerhead's cephalofoil to fraudulently sell it as ordinary shark is a notable ethnographic detail.

results.title_inferred: On the Huatzin and the Aura (King Vulture): two Mexican birds and their uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVII
Del huatzín

El ave llamada huatzín en la Nueva España es casi tan grande como las gallinas de Indias; tiene el pico corvo, el pecho blanco que tira a amarillo, las alas y la cola manchadas por intervalos con unas rayas anchas como el dedo pulgar, de color blanco y rubio; la espalda y la parte más allá del cuello de un color leonado tirante un poco a pardo oscuro, y algunas tienen lo mismo desde la cabeza al pecho; las uñas negras y las piernas pardas. Tienen una cresta compuesta de plumas de color blanco tirante a amarillo, y por los lados, hacia la espalda, son las plumas de color negro. Mantiénense de culebras y lagartijas; da una grande y vehemente voz con un grande aullido, y la tienen los indios por mal agüero. Vive en regiones calientes y anda por los árboles cercanos a los ríos. Picando con los huesos desta ave cualquiera parte del cuerpo humano que padezca dolor, se lo quita, y el sahumerio de sus plumas restituye el entendimiento a los que por alguna enfermedad quedaron algo faltos dél.

CAPITULO XVIII
De la aura

Llaman en la Nueva España cozcauhtli a una ave que los mexicanos nombran aura y dicen que es la reina de todas las aves. Es tan grande como las gallinas de Creta; tiene todo el cuerpo leonado y negro, fuera del cuello y las partes vecinas al pecho, que tiran de negro a bermejo; las alas, junto al nacimiento, son negras, y lo demás, de color ceniciento mezclado, por la parte de arriba, de leonado y negro; el pico rojo hacia el fin y semejante al de los papagayos; las uñas corvas, los ojos negros con la pupila leonada y los párpados bermejos. La frente de color de sangre y con muchos pliegues y arrugas, las cuales suele estirar y deshacer, en que se parece a los gallipavos; tiene unos pelos largos y retorcidos, semejantes al cabello de los negros; la cola de águila, por la parte inferior cenicienta y por la de fuera negra. Susténtase de culebras y otras sabandijas semejantes, que caza, de carnes mortecinas y de otras inmundicias. Es ave que apetece lo alto y nunca deja el vuelo; resiste con gran fuerza a los vientos, y suele estarse contra ellos sin moverse de un lugar gran rato. Su carne no es buena de comer.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVII
On the Huatzin

The bird called huatzin in New Spain is nearly as large as the turkeys [gallinas de Indias, lit. 'hens of the Indies']; it has a curved beak, a white breast tending toward yellow, and wings and tail marked at intervals with broad bands as wide as a thumb, of white and tawny color; the back and the part beyond the neck are of a tawny color inclining somewhat to dark brown, and some individuals show the same coloring from the head to the breast; the claws are black and the legs brown. They have a crest composed of feathers that are whitish tending toward yellow, and along the sides toward the back the feathers are black. They subsist on snakes and lizards; they emit a loud and forceful cry with a great howling sound, and the Indians regard them as a bad omen. They live in hot regions and frequent the trees near rivers. Pricking with the bones of this bird any part of the human body that suffers pain removes the pain; and the fumigation [sahumerio] of its feathers restores the understanding of those who, through some illness, have been left somewhat lacking in it.

CHAPTER XVIII
On the Aura

In New Spain they call cozcauhtli a bird that the Mexicans name aura, and they say it is the queen of all birds. It is as large as the hens of Crete [i.e., domestic fowl]; the whole body is tawny and black, except for the neck and the parts near the breast, which shade from black to crimson; the wings near their base are black, and the rest is ashen in color, mixed on the upper side with tawny and black; the beak is red toward the tip and resembles that of parrots; the talons are curved, the eyes black with a tawny pupil, and the eyelids crimson. The forehead is blood-red with many folds and wrinkles, which it habitually stretches and smooths out, in which respect it resembles turkeys [gallipavos]; it has long, twisted hairs resembling the hair of Africans [negros]; the tail is like that of an eagle, ashen on the underside and black on the outside. It feeds on snakes and other similar creatures that it hunts, on carrion, and on other refuse. It is a bird that seeks great heights and never ceases flying; it resists winds with great force, and it is accustomed to hover against them without moving from one spot for a long while. Its flesh is not good to eat.

entities_mentioned.name: huatzín
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hoatzin (Opisthocomus hoazin)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — crest, tawny-and-black coloring, riparian habitat, diet of reptiles, loud call — fits the hoatzin closely. The name 'huatzin' is the direct etymological source of the common English name.

entities_mentioned.name: cozcauhtli / aura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: King Vulture (Sarcoramphus papa)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name cozcauhtli, the description of the colorful, wrinkled, red-and-black head resembling a turkey's, the tawny-and-black body, diet of carrion and reptiles, and soaring flight all correspond to the King Vulture. Called 'queen of all birds' here.

results.translator_notes: Medical/ritual uses are recorded for the huatzin only. (1) Condition treated: bodily pain (unspecified). Preparation: bones of the bird used to prick [picar] the painful area. (2) Condition treated: cognitive or mental impairment following illness. Preparation: fumigation [sahumerio] with the bird's feathers. — OCR errors silently corrected include: 'gollinas' → 'gallinas', 'Manco' → 'blanco', 'jiadezca' → 'padezca', 'coz-ctiauhtli' → 'cozcauhtli', 'pedio' → 'pecho', 'ojíg' → 'ojos', 'pfc pados' → 'párpados', '4' (passim in ch. XVIII) → 'de', 'b alio' → 'lo alto', 'estarse' reconstructed from split line. The reference to 'gallinas de Creta' in ch. XVIII likely means ordinary domestic hens (gallinas de Castilla/Creta used loosely) rather than a specific Cretan breed.

results.title_inferred: The Quenti (Hummingbird): Description, Habits, Hibernation, and Use
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVII
Del quenti

El quenti es el menor de los pájaros que se hallan en estas Indias, porque es tan pequeño, que teniéndolo en la mano y cerrando el puño con él dentro, no hace más bulto que si fuera una bellota de encina; y pelado, viene a quedar poco mayor que un moscardón; porque, pelada, su cabeza es del tamaño de un garbanzo. Para ser tan pequeño, tiene mucha pluma, con la cual, de la punta del pico a la cola, tiene de largo el ancho de cuatro dedos, y extendidas las alillas, tiene de punta a punta seis dedos atravesados. El pico es tan largo como la mitad de su cuerpecillo, muy delgado y negro; las piernecillas muy delgadas, y tan cortas, que apenas tienen de donde echar mano, y las uñillas negras. Las plumas son pintadas de muchos y hermosos colores, de las cuales los indios de la Nueva España hacen aquellas imágenes tan preciosas, componiendo unos colores con otros con tanta sutileza y artificio, que admira.

Tiene este pajarito una naturaleza prodigiosa, y es que en las tierras donde se agostan las flores no vive más tiempo de lo que ellas duran, de cuya melosidad se mantiene, sustentándose sobre ellas sin asentarse en el árbol, sino moviendo velozmente sus alillas, se está parado en el aire un buen rato; y en pasándose el tiempo de las flores, se allega a un pino, o a otros árboles, guiado de su natural instinto, y asiéndose con el pico, se queda colgado por tiempo de seis meses, poco más o menos, y en comenzando por la primavera a florecer las plantas, torna él a cobrar vida o despertar de aquel largo sueño. Por ser tan admirable la propiedad deste pajarillo, se me había hecho difícil de creer, aunque lo había leído en autores y oído a muchas personas; pero residiendo yo en la ciudad de México e inquiriendo yo si hallaba testigo de vista, vine a saber de cierto que en el pueblo de Tepozotlán, cinco leguas de México, que es doctrina de la Compañía de Jesús, trujo una vez un indio a uno de nuestros padres un ramo de árbol en que estaba clavado del pico y muerto o dormido un pajarillo déstos; el cual guardó el padre en su aposento y vió que, en siendo tiempo, revivió, y desasiéndose de la rama, se fue volando. El cual suceso tomó el padre por argumento para predicar a los indios el misterio de la Resurrección.

Es cosa de ver la facilidad con que los indios de la Nueva España cazan estos pajarillos y los demás de plumas preciosas, que es desta manera: asiéntase un indio a la orilla de un arroyo, donde acuden a beber, y cubierto entre ramas, saca de entre ellas una varilla larga untada con liga, y tiénela tendida un poco levantada del agua, y los pajarillos que acuden a beber, se sientan en ella y quedan presos, y desta manera, en breve espacio, coge gran cantidad déllos. Dicen que, bebidos los polvos destos pajarillos, curan la gota coral. Hállanse algunas diferencias déllos, que sólo se distinguen en el tamaño y en los colores de sus plumas.
En la lengua del Perú se llama este pajarillo, quenti, y en la mexicana, huitsitzil.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVII
On the Quenti

The quenti is the smallest of the birds found in these Indies, for it is so tiny that if one holds it in the hand and closes the fist around it, it makes no more bulk than an acorn of the oak [bellota de encina]; and plucked, it ends up scarcely larger than a horsefly [moscardón], since when plucked its head is no bigger than a chickpea [garbanzo]. Small as it is, it has a great deal of plumage, by means of which, from the tip of its beak to its tail, it measures in length the breadth of four fingers, and with its little wings spread, it measures six fingers from tip to tip. The beak is as long as half its little body, very slender and black; its tiny legs are very slender and so short that one can scarcely get a grip on them, and its little claws are black. Its feathers are painted with many beautiful colors, from which the Indians of New Spain make those most precious images [featherwork], combining one color with another with such delicacy and artistry as to inspire wonder.

This little bird has a prodigious nature, namely that in lands where the flowers wither [se agostan] it lives no longer than the flowers last, sustaining itself on their nectar [melosidad], hovering over them without alighting on the tree but rather moving its little wings so swiftly that it remains suspended in the air for a good while. And when the season of flowers has passed, guided by its natural instinct it draws near to a pine or other trees, and clinging by its beak, it hangs there for approximately six months, and when in springtime the plants begin to flower again, it recovers life and awakens from that long sleep. Because the property of this little bird is so admirable, I had found it difficult to believe, though I had read of it in authors and heard it from many persons. But while residing in the city of Mexico and inquiring whether I might find an eyewitness, I came to know for certain that in the town of Tepozotlán, five leagues from Mexico City, which is a mission [doctrina] of the Society of Jesus, an Indian once brought to one of our Fathers a tree branch on which one of these little birds was attached by its beak, dead or asleep. The Father kept it in his room and saw that, when the time came, it revived, and releasing itself from the branch, flew away. This event the Father took as a theme for preaching to the Indians the mystery of the Resurrection.

It is a thing to behold, the ease with which the Indians of New Spain catch these little birds and others of precious plumage, which is done in the following manner: an Indian seats himself at the edge of a stream where the birds come to drink, and concealed among branches, he extends from among them a long stick smeared with birdlime [liga], holding it stretched out a little above the water; the little birds that come to drink alight upon it and are caught, and in this way, within a short time, he takes a great quantity of them. It is said that the powders [polvos] of these little birds, taken as a drink, cure epilepsy [gota coral, lit. 'coral gout']. Several varieties of them are found, distinguished only by their size and the colors of their plumage.
In the language of Peru this little bird is called quenti, and in the Mexican language, huitsitzil.

entities_mentioned.name: quenti (also huitsitzil in Nahuatl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hummingbird (family Trochilidae); the Nahuatl name huitsitzil (modern: huitzilin) confirms this. The Peruvian quenti is also a hummingbird, likely referring to multiple species.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes hovering flight, nectar feeding, and a folklore tradition of seasonal torpor ('hibernation') lasting six months, during which the bird clings to a branch by its beak. This reflects a widely reported indigenous belief about hummingbird torpor; while hummingbirds do enter nightly or short-term torpor, six-month hibernation is not scientifically documented. The featherwork tradition (plumaria) described is well-attested for Aztec/Mexica craftwork.

entities_mentioned.name: gota coral
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epilepsy (the term 'gota coral' was the standard 17th-century Spanish term for epilepsy or falling sickness)

entities_mentioned.name: liga (birdlime)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viscous adhesive substance applied to sticks to trap birds; commonly made from plant resins or mistletoe berries.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as a hunting method: a lime-smeared stick held over water where hummingbirds come to drink.

entities_mentioned.name: Tepozotlán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tepotzotlán, a town in the State of Mexico approximately 40 km north of Mexico City, site of a notable Jesuit mission and college.

medical_extraction.preparation_method: The powders [polvos] of the bird are drunk (oral administration of ground/powdered dried bird).

results.translator_notes: The footnotes in the source text are editorial annotations by a later editor (not Cobo himself) and have been contextually noted but not incorporated into the main translation. Footnote 9 mentions that Chilean ladies reportedly 'advance the resurrection' of hummingbirds by warming them in their bosoms, hence the local name 'resurrected birds' (pájaros resucitados). The OCR rendered several words with broken characters (e.g., 'ípie' for 'que', 'peclar' for 'quedar', 'ceceño' for 'pequeño', 'aedipcio»' as a footnote artifact); these have been silently corrected where unambiguous. The word 'melosidad' (honeyed sweetness, i.e., nectar) is a period technical term rendered as 'nectar' in the translation with the original noted. The description of hummingbird hibernation reflects a genuine colonial-era natural history tradition and is not mere fantasy; Cobo presents it critically, noting his initial skepticism and citing a Jesuit eyewitness account from Tepozotlán.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVI
De la tandia

La tandia es el pájaro que los españoles llamamos carpintero, porque anda siempre dando grandes golpes en los troncos de los árboles con el pico, que lo tiene largo, recio y agudo. Hállanse tres maneras de tandias distintas solo en el tamaño; las mayores son como grandes papagayos, otras mucho menores, y las medianas no mayores que tordos. Es la tandia pintada de todos estos colores, negro, blanco, azul, amarillo, verde y colorado, por este orden: el cuerpo tiene pintado a través o carreras de negro claro y verde cada una por sí, y el verde tirante algo a amarillo; el cuello y vientre blanco; una mancha en forma de corona sobre la cabeza, colorada o amarilla o azul, y algunas plumas coloradas encima de la cola. Andan estos pájaros a saltos, y suben por el tronco de los árboles saltando; no se asientan y paran en los ramos de los árboles, como las demás aves, sino agarrados con las uñas en los troncos, por lisa y dura que tenga la corteza; y aunque vayan volando, se pegan y agarran desta suerte. Hacen sus nidos en los troncos de los árboles y palmas, y dan tan grandes golpes con los picos en ellos, así para abrir el nido (que lo hacen con el pico como si fuera escoplo), como para hacer salir los gusanillos y hormigas que comen, que se oyen muy lejos, y no pocas veces engañan a los que los oyen, que piensan ser golpes de hacha con que está cortando leña alguna persona.

Es la tandia pájaro que se domestica, y tan animoso, que uno que se criaba manso en una casa, sucedía que llegándosele los gatos a quitarle la comida, arremetía a ellos y, a picadas, hacía huir a tres gatos juntos; y si le daban algunas uñaradas, no se acobardaba ni huía, antes, con nuevo brío, los embestía sin volver las espaldas, y los seguía haciéndoles huir, y luego cantaba la victoria. Otra cosa se notó en este pájaro doméstico: que trayendo otros pajarillos pequeños de su misma casta que no sabían comer, él mismo, como si fuera su padre o madre, les daba de comer por su pico; y subiéndose en un árbol frutal que había en el patio de la casa donde se criaba, derribaba cantidad de fruta, y bajándose del árbol, la partía con su pico y daba a comer a los pequeñuelos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVI
Of the Tandia

The tandia is the bird that we Spaniards call the carpintero [carpenter], because it goes about constantly striking great blows on the trunks of trees with its beak, which is long, stout, and sharp. Three kinds of tandia are found, differing only in size: the largest are as big as great parrots, others are much smaller, and the medium-sized ones are no larger than thrushes. The tandia is marked with all of the following colours — black, white, blue, yellow, green, and red — in this order: the body is painted across in bands [carreras] of light black and green, each distinct, the green inclining somewhat toward yellow; the neck and belly are white; a crown-shaped patch on the head, which may be red, yellow, or blue; and some red feathers above the tail. These birds move about by hopping, and climb the trunks of trees by leaping; they do not perch and rest on the branches of trees as other birds do, but cling with their claws to the trunks, however smooth and hard the bark may be; and even when in flight they attach and grip themselves in this manner. They make their nests in the trunks of trees and palms, and strike such great blows with their beaks upon them — both to hollow out the nest (which they carve with the beak as if it were a chisel) and to drive out the small grubs and ants they feed upon — that the sound carries very far, and more than once has deceived those who hear it into thinking it the blows of an axe by which some person is chopping firewood.

The tandia is a bird that can be tamed, and one of such bold spirit that a specimen raised tame in a household, when cats approached to take its food, would charge at them and, with its pecks, put three cats to flight at once; and if they dealt it some scratches with their claws, it was not daunted nor did it flee; rather, with renewed vigour, it attacked them without turning its back, pursuing them until they fled, and then sang its victory. Another thing was observed in this domestic bird: when small fledglings of its own kind that did not yet know how to eat were brought to it, it would feed them from its own beak, as if it were their father or mother; and climbing up into a fruit tree that stood in the courtyard of the house where it was raised, it would knock down a quantity of fruit, and coming back down from the tree, would split it with its beak and give it to the little ones to eat.

entities_mentioned.name: tandia (also: carpintero)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Woodpecker (family Picidae); likely one of several Neotropical woodpecker species, possibly Campephilus or Dryocopus spp. given the large size and vivid coloration described
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes three size variants. The footnote in the source text notes that in the Quijos region and along the Napo River, Quechua-speaking indigenous people use the term 'Siccha' for Tanagra and related birds — this footnote appears to be an editorial addition by a later annotator and does not match Cobo's own text, which clearly describes a woodpecker. The footnote may be misplaced or erroneous. No medicinal or pharmacological content in this segment.

results.translator_notes: The editorial footnote (marked '(11)' in the source) associating the tandia with Tanagra (tanagers) appears to be a later annotator's gloss and is almost certainly incorrect in this context — Cobo's description is unambiguously that of a woodpecker (Picidae). The footnote has been preserved in the cleaned original for completeness but is not incorporated into the translation. The word 'carreras' (literally 'lanes' or 'runs') is used to describe the banded plumage pattern; translated as 'bands.' OCR errors silently corrected include: 'i(iTidias¡' → 'tandias', 'qixe' → 'que', 'hacía' (spelled 'bacía'), 'haciéndoles' (spelled 'baeéndoles'), 'qué' → 'que', 'dahan' → 'daban', 'pajarillos' (spelled 'pajarillos'), and others.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIX
De la zuquianga

La zuquianga es un pájaro del taño de una polla grande, o muy poco menor que una gallina, de color negro, con algo de blanco y azul; tiene el pico tan grande casi como todo su cuerpo, largo un jeme y ancho dos dedos; el cual es muy vistoso por su lustre y colores; porque es listado de negro y amarillo, y dél hacen muy curiosos frasquillos de pólvora. Por tener este pájaro tan desproporcionado pico no lo puede mandar bien para comer, si bien no le falta industria para esto, porque, con la punta dél coje la comida, y alzándola en alto, la recoge con el pico abierto y se la engulle. Su carne es buena de comer. Llámanlo zuquianga en el obispado de Quito; y tocón, en el de Santa Cruz de la Sierra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On the Zuquianga

The zuquianga is a bird the size of a large hen, or only slightly smaller than a chicken, black in color with some white and blue. Its beak is nearly as large as its entire body, a hand-span [jeme] in length and two fingers in width; it is very striking on account of its sheen and colors, being striped in black and yellow, and from it [the beak] people make very curious little powder-flasks [frasquillos de pólvora]. Because this bird has so disproportionately large a beak, it cannot manage it well for eating; nevertheless it is not without ingenuity in this regard, for with the tip of the beak it picks up its food, and tossing it upward, catches it in the open beak and swallows it. Its flesh is good to eat. It is called zuquianga in the bishopric of Quito, and tocón in that of Santa Cruz de la Sierra.

entities_mentioned.name: zuquianga (also: tocón)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toucan (family Ramphastidae), likely Ramphastos sp. or a closely related genus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — large colorful beak striped black and yellow, approximately the length of the bird's body, used as powder-flasks — is consistent with a large toucan. The regional name 'tocón' also persists in some Andean Spanish dialects for toucans or related birds. The tossing feeding behavior described is a well-known characteristic of toucans.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'ño' → 'taño' (i.e., 'tamaño', size); 'ne^o' → 'negro'; 'dedo»' → 'dedos'; 'ca¬ lores' → 'colores'; 'fras^ líos' → 'frasquillos'; 'pájare' → 'pájaro'; 'paed®' → 'puede'; 'fe' → 'le'; 'cc^' → 'con'; 'a»' → 'alzándola'; 'pie®' → 'pico'. The word 'jeme' is a traditional Spanish unit of measure approximately equal to the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger (roughly 8–10 cm), retained in brackets in the translation. The passage is zoological in nature, not medical/botanical; accordingly, no drug or treatment extraction applies.

results.title_inferred: The Paujia (Crested Curassow) of Tierra Firme and Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
De la paujia

En el reino de Tierra Firme llaman paujia a cierta ave grande, que también se halla en las tierras calientes del Perú, y los españoles la nombran pava de la tierra, porque se parece algo a los pavos reales de España, aunque no hace rueda con ellos, ni sus plumas son de aquel lustre y colores. Hay dos o tres especies destas pavas: unas son coronadas, y por esto las solemos llamar pavas reales, y las otras, no. La coronada es de hermoso parecer, tan grande como dos gallinas, de color negro con algunas plumas blancas en el pecho, piernas negras y pico colorado. Tiene sobre la cabeza una cresta, corona o rosa muy galana, que comienza desde encima del pico; fórmase de unas plumitas pequeñas relucientes y encrespadas, del largor de medio jeme, poco más o menos. La rosa es toda negra, y algunas tienen entreveradas algunas plumillas blancas; son estas plumitas muy preciadas para penachos y plumajes de sombreros.

Es la paujia ave silvestre y vuela mucho por los árboles; pero amánsase con facilidad y se hace casera; y domesticada no vuela como antes, y su carne es muy buena de comer. Quítanle aquella rosa o penacho de tres a tres meses, y en aquel tiempo le vuelve a nacer y crecer. Es muy para reparar, que en quitándoles a estas aves la corona, quedan como avergonzadas y cobardes, sin aquel brío y lozanía que con ella tenían; tanto, que habiendo en cierta casa algunas domésticas, notaron los della que perseguía una a otra y la llevaba vencida, y en quitando el penacho a la que antes era vencedora, se rindió a su contraria y huía della; y la que antes era perseguida cobró tanto brío, que no dejaba sosegar a su seguidora, como vengándose della. Otras pavas hay déstas negras y pardas, que tienen gran vuelo, con el pico y piernas amarillas, y se parecen mucho a la primera, sólo que carecen de corona.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LV
Of the Paujia

In the kingdom of Tierra Firme [the Spanish Main], a certain large bird is called the paujia; it is also found in the warm lowlands of Peru. The Spaniards call it pava de la tierra [turkey-hen of the land] because it bears some resemblance to the peacocks [pavos reales] of Spain, although it does not spread its tail as they do, nor are its feathers of that same sheen and coloring. There are two or three species of these pavas: some are crested, and for this reason we tend to call them pavas reales [royal turkey-hens], while the others are not. The crested one is handsome in appearance, as large as two hens, black in color with some white feathers on the breast, black legs, and a red bill. On its head it bears a very elegant crest, crown, or rosette that begins above the bill; it is formed of small, shining, curled little feathers roughly half a jeme [approximately two to three inches] in length. The rosette is entirely black, though some individuals have a few small white feathers interspersed among them; these little feathers are highly prized for plumes and hat ornaments.

The paujia is a wild bird that flies freely among the trees, but it is easily tamed and becomes a household bird; once domesticated it no longer flies as before, and its flesh is very good to eat. The rosette or plume is plucked from it every three months, and within that time it grows back again. It is well worth noting that when these birds are deprived of their crown, they become as if ashamed and cowardly, losing all the vigor and spirit they had while wearing it. So much so that in a certain household where several tame ones were kept, the inhabitants observed that one was pursuing another and consistently getting the better of it; but when the plume was removed from the one that had previously been the victor, it submitted to its rival and fled from it, while the one that had previously been the pursued gained such boldness that it gave its former pursuer no rest, as if taking revenge upon it. There are other pavas of this kind, black and brown, that fly widely, with yellow bills and legs; they closely resemble the first type, except that they lack the crown.

entities_mentioned.name: paujia (coronada / pava real)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crax alector (Black Curassow) or Crax rubra (Great Curassow); the crested description most closely matches Crax alector or possibly Pauxi pauxi (Helmeted Curassow)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of the elaborate curled crest beginning above the bill and the red bill on a black bird is consistent with Pauxi pauxi (Helmeted Curassow) or Crax alector. The genus name Pauxi likely derives directly from the vernacular 'paujia/paují.' The 'pava real' epithet reflects the crowned variant.

entities_mentioned.name: otras pavas negras y pardas (without crown, yellow bill and legs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Penelope or Aburria species (guans, family Cracidae), or an uncrested Crax species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes these from the crowned paujia by their lack of crest and their yellow bill and legs. Could refer to several cracid species common to Tierra Firme and lowland Peru.

results.translator_notes: The word 'corona' (lit. 'crown') and 'rosa' (lit. 'rose') are both used by Cobo to describe the crest ornament; 'penacho' is also employed (lit. 'plume' or 'tuft'). The unit of measure 'jeme' is the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when extended, roughly 7–8 cm. The OCR rendered 'pát?as reales' (evidently 'pavas reales') and 'carecen de / roña' (evidently 'carecen de corona,' split across a line break); both have been silently corrected as unambiguous. The behavioral anecdote about dominance and the crest is a notable early natural-history observation on social hierarchy in birds. No medicinal or pharmaceutical content appears in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On the Rhea (American Ostrich) of Peru and surrounding provinces
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIX
Del avestruz

En los páramos y punas de la Sierra del Perú, y mucho más en las provincias del Cuyo, Tucumán y Santa Cruz de la Sierra, se crían gran suma de avestruces. Son las mayores aves de toda la América, y no de tan finas plumas como ellos, a cuya causa no se aprovechan dellas los españoles en esta tierra para plumajes, sino que las gastan en hacer curiosos quitasoles y plumeros para sacudir los altares y el polvo de las imágenes, para lo cual son por extremo buenas y muy preciadas; y para que salgan los plumeros más galanos, suelen teñirlas de varios colores. No se levantan los avestruces del suelo, mas que, a vuela pie, corren muy ligeramente. Cázanlos los indios con perros, así para comer su carne, como para aprovecharse de su pluma. Si tomaran la carrera derecha cuando van huyendo, no habría caballo ni galgo que los alcanzara, pero no corren así, sino dando muchas vueltas a una y otra parte, que es causa de que, corriendo el cazador derecho, los alcance. A los que cogen pequeños, llévanlos los indios a sus pueblos, y cortándoles el dedo de en medio, para que no corran, los domestican y crían a manadas como aves caseras, por el provecho que sacan de su carne y pluma.

Estando yo en la provincia de Chuquito, vi dos que, habiéndolos cogido en la puna pequeños, se criaban caseros, y siendo ya del tamaño de pavos, mató al uno un perro. Tienen los avestruces un muy particular instinto en criar sus hijos, y es que cuando han juntado los huevos en el nido (que de ordinario es cada nidada de veinte o treinta), antes de empollarlos, apartan dos o tres para que se pudran y no se empollen, y en sacando los hijuelos, o un poco antes, los quiebran; a los cuales acuden muchas moscas, que comen los polluelos, con las cuales se sustentan dos o tres días, y luego los sacan a pacer hierba, que es el sustento destas aves, y luego los llevan donde hay tierra salobre para que coman della. Por donde, a un campo que hay desta tierra en la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra, adonde acuden muchos avestruces, llaman los indios chiriguanos, en su lengua, yanduhiguá (14), que quiere decir tierra que comen los avestruces. Su unto es provechoso para desentumir y alargar cualquiera miembro encogido por alguna enfermedad y para los brazos inflamados y doloridos por causa de sangría; quita las opilaciones o durezas del estómago, hígado y bazo, y resuelve y ablanda los escirros o cualquiera dureza. Llámase el avestruz, en la lengua general del Perú, suri (15).

(14) Niandú biguá o biguá.
(15) Nandú, Niandú o Ñandú, en el guaraní o tupí, y Chokjue, en chileno o araucano.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LIX
On the Rhea [avestruz, "ostrich"]

In the moorlands [páramos] and high plateaus [punas] of the Andean range of Peru, and still more abundantly in the provinces of Cuyo, Tucumán, and Santa Cruz de la Sierra, great numbers of rheas [avestruces] are raised. They are the largest birds in all of America, though their feathers are not so fine as those of the [Old World] ostrich, for which reason the Spaniards in this land do not use them as plumes for adornment; rather, they employ them to make curious parasols [quitasoles] and feather-dusters for sweeping the altars and removing dust from religious images, for which purpose they are extremely good and highly prized. In order to make the dusters more elegant, the feathers are commonly dyed in various colors. Rheas do not rise from the ground in flight, but run with great swiftness on foot [a vuela pie]. The Indians hunt them with dogs, both to eat their flesh and to make use of their feathers. Were the birds to run in a straight line when fleeing, neither horse nor greyhound [galgo] could overtake them; but they do not run thus, instead turning many times to one side and the other, which causes the hunter, running straight, to catch up with them. When chicks are caught, the Indians bring them back to their villages and, cutting off the middle toe so that they cannot run, tame and raise them in flocks like domestic fowl, for the benefit they derive from their flesh and feathers.

While I was in the province of Chuquito, I saw two birds that had been caught as chicks on the high plateau and were being raised as domestic animals; when they had already reached the size of turkeys, a dog killed one of them. Rheas have a most singular instinct in raising their young: when they have gathered their eggs in the nest (which ordinarily numbers twenty or thirty eggs per clutch), before brooding them they set two or three aside to rot and go unhatched; then, just as the chicks are hatching, or a little before, they break these rotten eggs, which attract great numbers of flies that the chicks feed upon, thereby sustaining themselves for two or three days. Afterwards the chicks are led out to graze on grass, which is the principal sustenance of these birds, and then taken to places where there is brackish [salobre] earth for them to eat. For this reason, a stretch of such ground in the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, where many rheas congregate, is called by the Chiriguano Indians in their language yanduhiguá (14), meaning "the earth that the rheas eat." The fat [unto] of the rhea is beneficial for relieving numbness and restoring any limb that has contracted due to illness, and for arms that are inflamed and painful as a result of bloodletting [sangría]; it removes obstructions [opilaciones] or hardnesses of the stomach, liver, and spleen, and resolves and softens scirrhous growths [escirros] or any hardness. The rhea is called, in the general language [lingua franca] of Peru, suri (15).

(14) Niandú biguá or biguá.
(15) Nandú, Niandú, or Ñandú in Guaraní or Tupí, and Chokjue in Chilean or Araucanian.

entities_mentioned.name: avestruz / suri / Ñandú / Nandú / Chokjue
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhea americana (greater rhea) or Rhea pennata (lesser rhea / Darwin's rhea), family Rheidae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'avestruz' (ostrich) loosely for the South American rhea. 'Suri' in Quechua typically refers specifically to the puna rhea (Rhea pennata / Pterocnemia pennata), while 'Ñandú' in Guaraní refers to Rhea americana. Both species are plausible given the geographic range described (Andean punas through Tucumán and Santa Cruz de la Sierra). The medical use described is for the rendered fat [unto].

results.translator_notes: Medical/pharmacological extraction — Rhea fat (unto de avestruz): (1) Name used: unto de avestruz / unto del suri. (2) Modern identification: rendered rhea fat. (3) Conditions treated: numbness and contracture of limbs from illness; inflammation and pain of the arms following bloodletting [sangría]; obstructions or hardening of the stomach, liver, and spleen [opilaciones]; scirrhous tumors or any induration [escirros, durezas]. (4) Preparation method: not explicitly stated; 'unto' implies the rendered animal fat applied topically or possibly internally. — OCR errors corrected silently: 'dUs' → 'cias' (provincias); 'resírwccs' → 'avestruces'; 'k' → 'la'; 'mmo' → 'como'; 'ntremo' → 'extremo'; 'go/go' → 'galgo' (greyhound); 'á' → 'a'. The footnote numeral (14) in source reads 'Niandú biguá o hihuá' — likely 'biguá' in both instances (OCR artifact); retained as 'biguá' in cleaned text. Cobo's account of rheas setting aside rotten eggs to attract flies as food for chicks is a piece of early natural history observation of uncertain accuracy.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO
De las avispas

En la primera parte deste libro irán los animales imperfectos, en cuya producción y nacimiento no siempre interviene perfecta generación, cual es la que se hace de un viviente en otro su semejante en especie, sino que todos ellos pueden proceder de putrefacción alterada de las calidades de los elementos, e influencias celestes, que es generación imperfecta; dado caso que algunos nazcan por vía de entrambas generaciones, como vemos que acaece a los ratones y a otros desta categoría. Y a esta causa me pareció no haber necesidad de hacer en este lugar capítulo distinto de los animales deste jaez que se hallan en estas Indias semejantes a los que nacen en España, como acostumbro hacer escribiendo de las plantas y animales perfectos; porque todos los deste género, como viles e imperfectos, dondequiera los produce la tierra de suyo.

Y comenzando por las avispas, digo, que se hallan en toda la América de la misma casta que las de Europa. Labran sus inútiles y secos panales, y son tan nocivas con su aguijón como allá. Nacen principalmente en las tierras calientes, como vemos en esta costa del Perú; más, en las muy frías, como son las provincias del Collao, se hallan muy pocas. El remedio que he visto usar contra ellas en los valles de los Llanos del Perú, es esperar el invierno, y por las mañanas irles derribando sus panales, que por estar entumecidas con el frío, no hacen daño ni se mueven del lugar en que caen; con que, sin riesgo alguno de que piquen, las van matando. Llaman los indios peruanos a la avispa, uruncoy.
results.translation:
CHAPTER ONE
On Wasps

In the first part of this book will come the imperfect animals, in whose production and birth perfect generation does not always take part — that is to say, the kind by which one living being gives rise to another of the same species — but rather all of them may proceed from the putrefaction brought about by the altered qualities of the elements and celestial influences, which is imperfect generation; notwithstanding that some are born by means of both kinds of generation, as we see happens with mice and others of this category. For this reason it seemed to me that there was no need to devote a separate chapter here to the animals of this sort found in the Indies that resemble those born in Spain, as I customarily do when writing about plants and perfect animals; for all creatures of this kind, being base and imperfect, the earth produces them of itself wherever it may be.

Beginning then with wasps, I say that they are found throughout all of America and are of the same stock as those of Europe. They build their useless and dry honeycombs, and are as harmful with their sting as they are there. They are found principally in warm lands, as we see on this coast of Peru; but in very cold regions, such as the provinces of the Collao [Altiplano], very few are found. The remedy I have seen used against them in the valleys of the Llanos [coastal plains] of Peru is to wait for winter, and in the mornings to go about knocking down their combs; for, being numbed by the cold, the wasps do no harm and do not move from the spot where they fall, so that, without any risk of being stung, they are killed off one by one. The Peruvian Indians call the wasp uruncoy.

entities_mentioned.name: avispa (uruncoy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vespidae spp. (various wasp species, likely including Polybia and Polistes spp. in the American context)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo states these are the same stock as European wasps. The Quechua name recorded is 'uruncoy.' In warm coastal Peru, species of social wasps such as Polybia occidentalis are common. The cold-numbness control method described is an early recorded form of passive pest management.

entities_mentioned.name: ratones (mice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mus musculus or related Muridae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as an example of animals believed capable of arising by both spontaneous generation and natural reproduction, reflecting Aristotelian natural philosophy standard in the period.

results.translator_notes: The passage reflects classical Aristotelian doctrine of 'spontaneous generation' (generación imperfecta / generación espontánea), which Cobo uses to justify treating 'imperfect' animals as a distinct category not requiring region-by-region comparison. The term 'panales' here refers to wasp nests/combs, not honeybee combs, since Cobo explicitly calls them 'inútiles y secos' (useless and dry). The Quechua term 'uruncoy' is of ethnolinguistic interest and may warrant cross-referencing with Quechua lexicons. No medical or pharmacological content is present in this segment.
