results.original_text:
CAPITULO VII
De las mariposas

Puédense reducir a dos clases todas las diferencias que se hallan de mariposas, que son casi sin número: a la primera, las nocturnas, que no parecen de día sino de noche a la luz de la candela; y a la segunda, las que vuelan de día. De las primeras hay en esta tierra todas las especies que en Europa, y tienen la misma propiedad de revoletear alrededor de la luz hasta abrasarse en ella. Son casi todas de color ceniciento, y sólo difieren en el tamaño. Las menores que yo he visto son no mayores que pulgas, y de aquí para arriba las hay como mosquitos, como moscas, y hasta del tamaño y mayores que las comunes; unas son de hechura de palomitas, y déstas hay algunas de varios colores, pintadas y muy hermosas. Otras hay larguillas, de hechura de cigarras y grillos; y déstas de varios tamaños y colores. Una casta dellas son del grandor de moscas, de un hermoso verde; otras, mayores, de un negro atesado, y así otras de diferentes colores. En ninguna parte he experimentado que acudan más a la luz de la vela que en la provincia de Nicaragua, adonde me sucedió contar una noche más de doce diferencias dellas. Otras suelen venir a tiempos en esta ciudad de Lima, que son unos gusanillos rojos tamaño de un grano de cebada, con alas cenicientas y tan largas como su cuerpecillo, las cuales, después que se les han quemado las alas a la candela, viven algún tiempo sin ellas, porque el día siguiente se ven correr por la mesa muy ligeras, como hormigas. Las más comunes y de que hay mayor copia, son del tamaño de las ordinarias.

Del otro linaje de mariposas se hallan también muchas castas diferentes entre sí en el color y grandeza. Las más dellas son engendradas de los gusanos que se crían en la hortaliza: éstas son de muchos colores, unas blancas, otras pardas, azules, rojas, amarillas y algunas pintadas de varios colores; y todas ellas, imitando a sus progenitores, se mantienen de la hortaliza y legumbres, a que acuden a veces en tan grande número, que he visto en esta ciudad de Lima una huerta sembrada de coles agotada de tal manera por las mariposas, que no dejaron hoja verde más que los troncos mondos. Otras mariposas hay que no proceden deste principio, entre las cuales es muy para ver la que los indios peruanos llaman taparacu, la cual tiene cuatro alas, que, extendidas, viene a ser tan grande como la mano. Suelen algunas dellas, tendidas las alas, meterlas entre las hojas de un libro que quedan secas, y después sirven de registros; y son hermosísimas, porque sus alas, ultra de ser tan sutiles como tela de cebolla, hacen hermosísimos visos miradas por diferentes partes: por una muestran un color verde muy reluciente; por otra, un azul de cielo, y por otra, se muestran moradas y rojas. Llámase la mariposa, en la lengua peruana, pilpinto.

CAPITULO VIII
De las langostas, cigarras y grillos

Aunque son muchas las especies de animalillos deste jaez que se hallan en las Indias, los comprenderé todos en este capítulo, que son: langosta, cigarras y grillos; a éstos llaman los indios peruanos chillicutu, y son muy importunos, porque no sólo chillan en el campo, sino también dentro de las casas, con que suelen privarnos del sueño.

Fuera de las cigarras ordinarias, se hallan a cada paso otras muy prolongadas, con las alas como telillas de plata y de mucho vuelo. La langosta, enemigo capital de las legumbres, hace pocas veces daño en este reino del Perú; mas, en las provincias sus confinantes, es muy perjudicial, como es en el reino de Chile y provincias de Tucumán y Santa Cruz de la Sierra, adonde a tiempos se ven en tanta muchedumbre, que parecen nublados y talan los sembrados. Así las langostas como las cigarras son vianda familiar a muchas naciones de indios. Los de la provincia de Tucumán se vengan del daño que suelen recibir de las langostas, cogiendo cuantas pueden y hinchendo dellas después de secas las trojes que tenían preparadas para las semillas de que los defraudaron estas bestezuelas, y dellas van comiendo entre año. En la Nueva España suele algunos años comerse la langosta el giquilite, que es la planta de que se hace el añil. Llámase la langosta, en la lengua general del Perú, queche queche.
results.translation:
CHAPTER VII
On Butterflies

All the varieties of butterflies — which are nearly without number — can be reduced to two classes: the first, the nocturnal ones, which do not appear by day but only at night by candlelight; and the second, those that fly by day. Of the first kind, this land has all the species found in Europe, and they share the same habit of fluttering around a light until they are burned by it. Nearly all are ash-colored and differ only in size. The smallest I have seen are no larger than fleas; from there upward they range in size from gnats to flies, up to the size of the common [European] butterfly and larger. Some are shaped like little doves [palomitas], and among these there are some of various colors, spotted and very beautiful. Others are elongated, shaped like cicadas and crickets, and these come in various sizes and colors. One kind is the size of flies and a beautiful green; others, larger, are a deep polished black; and so on in different colors. I have nowhere observed them to gather more thickly around candlelight than in the province of Nicaragua, where one night I counted more than twelve distinct varieties. Others come at certain seasons to this city of Lima: these are tiny red worm-like creatures the size of a grain of barley, with ashen wings as long as their little bodies. After their wings have been singed by the candle, they live for some time without them, for the following day they can be seen running swiftly across the table, like ants. The most common and most abundant are the size of ordinary butterflies.

Of the other family of butterflies, many different kinds are likewise found, differing among themselves in color and size. Most of them are bred from the caterpillars [gusanos] that are raised in kitchen gardens: these come in many colors — some white, others brown, blue, red, yellow, and some spotted in various colors. All of them, following the habits of their forebears, feed on garden herbs and vegetables, and they sometimes arrive in such great numbers that I have seen, in this city of Lima, a garden planted with cabbages so thoroughly stripped by butterflies that they left no green leaf remaining, only the bare stalks. There are other butterflies that do not arise from this origin; among these, one most worth seeing is the one the Peruvian Indians call taparacu, which has four wings that, when spread, are as large as a man's hand. Some people are accustomed to pressing these, wings spread, between the pages of a book until they dry, and afterwards use them as bookmarks. They are exceedingly beautiful, for their wings — besides being as fine as onion-skin — produce most beautiful iridescent effects [visos] when viewed from different angles: from one angle they show a brilliant green; from another, a sky blue; and from another, they appear purple and red. The butterfly is called, in the Peruvian tongue, pilpinto.

CHAPTER VIII
On Locusts, Cicadas, and Crickets

Although there are many species of small creatures of this sort found in the Indies, I shall cover them all in this chapter, namely: the locust, cicadas, and crickets. The Peruvian Indians call crickets chillicutu; they are very troublesome, for they chirp not only in the fields but also inside houses, and thereby often rob us of our sleep.

Beyond the ordinary cicadas, one also finds at every step others of very elongated form, with wings like little sheets of silver and capable of great flight. The locust, the chief enemy of vegetables and crops, seldom causes damage in this kingdom of Peru; but in its neighboring provinces it is very harmful — as in the kingdom of Chile and the provinces of Tucumán and Santa Cruz de la Sierra — where at times they are seen in such multitudes that they resemble storm clouds and lay waste to the sown fields. Both locusts and cicadas are familiar food to many Indian nations. The people of the province of Tucumán take revenge for the damage the locusts regularly inflict upon them by catching as many as they can and, once dried, filling with them the same granaries that had been prepared for the seeds of which these little creatures had defrauded them; and from these stores they eat throughout the year [footnote 5]. In New Spain, some years the locust eats the giquilite [jiquilite], which is the plant from which indigo [añil] is made. The locust is called, in the general tongue [lengua general] of Peru, queche queche.

[Footnote 5]: As in North Africa, where they dry them in the sun, grind them, and eat this flour — even the nobles do so.

entities_mentioned.name: mariposa nocturna (nocturnal moth/butterfly)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various Lepidoptera: Heterocera (moths)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes multiple size classes of moths attracted to candlelight; the 'red worm-like' moth with ashen wings may refer to a micro-moth or possibly a wingless/brachypterous form.

entities_mentioned.name: taparacu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morpho butterfly (family Nymphalidae, genus Morpho) or similar large iridescent Neotropical butterfly
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as hand-sized with four wings showing iridescent green, blue, and purple/red hues from different angles — strongly consistent with Morpho spp., though other large iridescent Nymphalidae are possible. Quechua name taparacu noted.

entities_mentioned.name: pilpinto
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua general term for butterfly; Cobo gives it as the generic word for butterfly in the Peruvian tongue, not a species-specific name.

entities_mentioned.name: chillicutu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gryllidae spp. (crickets)
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name for cricket as recorded by Cobo; described as chirping both in fields and inside houses.

entities_mentioned.name: langosta / queche queche
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schistocerca cancellata or related South American locust species (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a major crop pest in Chile, Tucumán, and Santa Cruz de la Sierra; Quechua name queche queche given. Consumed as food by Tucumán Indians after drying. Also said to damage the jiquilite plant in New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: giquilite / jiquilite
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigofera suffruticosa or Indigofera guatemalensis (source of indigo dye)
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly identified by Cobo as the plant from which añil (indigo) is made; subject to locust damage in New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: cigarra (cicada)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cicadidae spp. (cicadas)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ordinary cicadas mentioned alongside an elongated variety with silvery, gossamer wings; both locusts and cicadas noted as food sources for indigenous peoples.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal or pharmaceutical content appears in these two chapters; they are purely natural-historical descriptions of insects. The term 'visos' (iridescent optical effects) has been rendered as 'iridescent effects' with the original in brackets for technical precision. The footnote, which appears to be an editorial annotation by a later editor (not Cobo himself), has been included at the end of the translation. OCR corrections made: 'todas' for 'toda»', 'primera' for 'pri*mera', 'de' for 'dé' (spurious accent throughout), 'en' for 'eH'', 'palomitas' for 'paZomitas', 'hinchendo' retained as period spelling of 'henchendo' (filling/stuffing). The passage on the taparacu butterfly pressed as bookmarks is a noteworthy ethnographic observation.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIII
De las orugas

Las orugas que nacen en las legumbres y en todo linaje de plantas, son unos gusanos que se engendran de las hojas verdes y se mantienen della: verdadera pestilencia de las huertas; los cuales crecen en tanta cantidad en las tierras calientes y templadas destas Indias, que a tiempos suelen destruir las plantas. Hállanse diferencias de orugas cuantas son las especies de legumbres y plantas; porque algunas veces he observado que difieren entre sí todas aquellas que son engendradas en diferentes linajes de legumbres y árboles. En la hoja de la granadilla se crían las menores que yo he visto, porque son unos gusanitos negros, casi tan delgados como un hilo, y no más largos que un piñón.

En la hoja del lúcumo se engendra otra suerte de gusanos poco más cortos que el dedo meñique, rubios y muy peludos; y a este modo en las demás plantas; en las cuales cargan a veces tantos, que las despojan de su hoja. Y es mucho de reparar que nunca la comen toda, sino que, royendo lo más tierno della, dejan enteras todas las venillas que tienen, de modo que parece después cada hoja destas una tela de araña, o una muy sutil redecita. No solamente son estos gusanos dañosos a las plantas, sino muchos dellos perniciosos al hombre; como son unos poco más cortos que ciempiés, más gruesos y vellosos, de cabeza colorada y todo el cuerpo pintado, que pican y son ponzoñosos.

Hállanse otros asimismo pintados con rayas a la larga y la cabeza negra, los cuales son tenidos por los peores. Los mayores que se crían deste género son ciertos gusanos del largor de un palmo y del grosor de un dedo, vellosos y con unos perfiles o rayas leonadas, de donde les nacen las piernas, las cuales y los cuernecillos son leonados, y el cuerpo del mismo color más oscuro. En ciertos árboles se engendra una manera de gusanos verdes tan largos y gruesos como un dedo y muy parecidos a los de la seda. Al gusano que roe las papas llaman los indios del Perú tkompa; y al que se come las mazorcas del maíz, hutuscuru; y desta manera tienen puestos a todos sus nombres. Cuando son viejos estos gusanos, se pegan a troncos de árboles y hacen cada uno su capullo, de que después salen las mariposas.

El gusano que se cría en el maíz, que es mediano, blanco y peludo, se convierte en mariposa desta forma: cuando siente llegarse el tiempo de su fin, dejando la mata del maíz, se pega en alguna pared o tronco de árbol y se está quedo sin moverse algunos días, en que poco a poco, a manera de fruta que va madurando, se va volviendo amarillo, y cuando ya todo él está deste color, levanta la cabeza, y con el hociquillo va arrancando todos los pelos de su cuerpo y formando dellos un pequeño capullo, en que queda encerrado, como lo hace el gusano de la seda; el cual acabado, si dentro de cuatro o cinco días lo quisiéramos romper, hallaremos dentro dél un gusanillo rojo, poco mayor que un piñón, sin cabeza ni distinción de miembros ni señal de vida; el cual, llegado su tiempo, en el capullo que no se ha roto, se convierte en una palomita o mariposa de color azul muy fino. A toda suerte de mariposas llaman laco los indios del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIII
On Caterpillars

The caterpillars [orugas] that appear on vegetables and on all manner of plants are worms [gusanos] generated from green leaves, upon which they also feed: a true pestilence of gardens. They multiply in such quantities in the warm and temperate lands of these Indies that at times they are wont to destroy the plants entirely. There are as many varieties of caterpillar as there are species of vegetables and plants; for I have at times observed that all those bred on different kinds of vegetables and trees differ from one another. On the leaf of the granadilla [passion fruit vine] are found the smallest I have ever seen, for they are tiny black worms, almost as thin as a thread and no longer than a pine nut.

On the leaf of the lúcumo [lucuma tree] there breeds another sort of worm, somewhat shorter than the little finger, tawny-colored and very hairy; and in like manner on the other plants, upon which they sometimes congregate in such numbers that they strip them bare of leaves. And it is well worth noting that they never consume the leaf entirely; rather, gnawing away the most tender parts, they leave intact all the small veins, so that each such leaf afterwards resembles a spider's web or a very fine little net. Not only are these worms harmful to plants, but many of them are also injurious to man; such as those somewhat shorter than centipedes [ciempiés], thicker and more hairy, with a red head and a variegated body, which sting and are venomous.

Others are found likewise marked with lengthwise stripes and a black head, and these are held to be the worst. The largest of this kind that are bred here are certain worms a hand-span [palmo] in length and a finger's breadth thick, hairy and bearing tawny-colored outlines or stripes at the points from which their legs emerge; these legs and their small horns are tawny, and the body is the same color but darker. On certain trees there breed a sort of green worms as long and as thick as a finger and very similar to silkworms. The worm that gnaws on potatoes the Indians of Peru call tkompa [topa]; and the one that eats the ears of maize [corn] they call hutuscuru; and in this fashion they have names assigned to all of them. When these worms are old, they attach themselves to the trunks of trees and each one makes its cocoon, from which the butterflies [mariposas] afterwards emerge.

The worm that breeds in maize—which is medium-sized, white, and hairy—transforms into a butterfly in the following manner: when it senses the approach of its end, it leaves the maize stalk, attaches itself to some wall or tree trunk, and remains still without moving for several days, during which, little by little, after the fashion of a fruit ripening, it gradually turns yellow. And when it is entirely of that color, it raises its head and with its little snout [hociquillo] proceeds to pull off all the hairs from its body, forming from them a small cocoon in which it remains enclosed, just as the silkworm does. Once this cocoon is finished, if we were to break it open within four or five days, we would find inside it a small red worm, little larger than a pine nut, without a head or any distinction of limbs, and showing no sign of life. This creature, when its time comes—in the cocoon that has not been broken open—transforms into a small moth or butterfly of a very fine blue color. The Indians of Peru call all manner of butterflies laco.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis or related Passiflora spp. (passion fruit vine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Host plant of the smallest caterpillars described; tiny black thread-like larvae.

entities_mentioned.name: lúcumo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria lucuma (lucuma tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Host plant of tawny, hairy caterpillars slightly shorter than the little finger.

entities_mentioned.name: tkompa (topa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a Symmetrischema or Tecia solanivora species (potato tuber moth larva) or similar potato pest; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name given by Peruvian Indians for the worm that gnaws on potatoes (papas). OCR renders the name as 'tkompa'; likely 'topa' or similar Quechua term. No confident modern taxonomic identification possible.

entities_mentioned.name: hutuscuru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Helicoverpa zea (corn earworm) or a related noctuid larva
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name for the worm that eats maize ears (mazorcas). Broader identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: gusano del maíz (maize worm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Helicoverpa zea or a related moth larva (family Noctuidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as medium-sized, white, and hairy; spins a cocoon from its own body hairs and emerges as a fine blue butterfly or moth. The spinning-of-hairs behavior is unusual and may indicate a lymantriid or arctiid larva rather than Helicoverpa.

entities_mentioned.name: laco
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua (Peruvian Indian) term for all butterflies and moths (mariposas).

entities_mentioned.name: gusano de la seda (silkworm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bombyx mori
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference point for cocoon-spinning behavior; not a New World entity.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Tkompa' in the OCR text is likely a misreading of a Quechua term for the potato pest; possible readings include 'topa,' 'ccopa,' or similar. Flagged with brackets in translation. 2. Cobo's account of the maize caterpillar pulling its own hairs to form a cocoon is a remarkable early naturalist observation of metamorphosis; the blue adult butterfly/moth it produces has not been confidently identified but the behavior suggests a woolly bear (Arctiidae/Erebidae) type larva. 3. 'Palmo' is a unit of measure roughly equivalent to the width of an outstretched hand (approx. 20–23 cm). 4. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'legnm^bres' → 'legumbres'; 'de la§ hojas' → 'de las hojas'; 'mantienen delU' → 'mantienen della'; 'huertas-' → 'huertas'; 'cantidad e»' → 'cantidad en'; 'especies de le.gumbres' → 'especies de legumbres'; 'algunas v^ces' → 'algunas veces'; 'pintadEoii' → 'pintados'; 'gusaiios' → 'gusanos'; 'gjazorcas' → 'mazorcas'; 'macera' → 'manera'; '^' superscripts indicating line breaks normalized. 5. The passage contains no medical/pharmacological content; no drug/preparation extraction is applicable.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Chapter XXVIII: On Mice and Rats of the New World",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XXVIII
De los ratones

Grandemente me admiro cuando leo algunos escritores de cosas de Indias, de ver se persuadiesen a creer y escribir algunas cosas desta tierra, que si de propósito buscaran qué decir en contra de lo que pasa en la materia de que tratan, no hablaran de otra manera. Y si bien es verdad que esta culpa se debe imputar más a los que los informaron que no a ellos, cuya intención fué sin duda de acertar, todavía debieran examinar con detenimiento lo que no sabían por vista de ojos, más que de oídas. Digo esto a propósito de que dice un escritor de cosas de Indias, que en todas ellas no había ratones hasta que los españoles vinieron, y que los que ahora hay, se han multiplicado de los que vinieron en un navío. Y otro historiador que imprimió después, queriendo corregir al primero, escribe que los ratones pequeños los había de antes, pero no los grandes. Y añade más, que los gatos que se han traído de España no acometen a éstos.

Todo lo cual es tan manifiestamente falso, que no tiene necesidad de otra prueba más de la que se toma de la experiencia cuotidiana. Porque, demás de que es cosa natural en todo el mundo engendrarse estos animalillos de la putrefacción de la tierra, en todas las lenguas de los indios tienen sus propios nombres. Y experimentamos cada día que, acabado de fabricar un navío en el astillero y echado al agua sin ratón alguno, dentro de poco tiempo se engendran más de los que quisieran los mareantes. Y en un páramo y desierto, donde antes no nacía ningún género de vivientes, ni aun las hierbas del campo, cuales son los arenales de los Llanos deste reino del Perú, que se edifique una casa o rancho, en muy breve tiempo remanecen en ella cantidad de ratones, no traídos de fuera, sino producidos allí.

Lo cierto y averiguado es que, así de los pequeños como de los grandes, hubo siempre en toda esta tierra tanta cantidad como ahora

results.title_inferred: The Amatinca: A Venomous Viper of Vilcabamba
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLI
De la amatinca

Llaman los indios peruanos amatinca a cierta especie de víboras que se crían en la provincia de Vilcabamba, diócesis de la ciudad de Cuzco; son pequeñas y verdes, y tienen en la frente una punta como aguja; las cuales desde los árboles, donde andan ordinariamente, se arrojan a cualquier animal que pasa, y con más velocidad que una saeta, lo clavan, quedando la venenosa serpiente muerta, como el animal herido, que también muere.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLI
On the Amatinca

The Peruvian Indians give the name amatinca to a certain species of viper found in the province of Vilcabamba, in the diocese of the city of Cuzco. They are small and green, and bear on their forehead a point like a needle. From the trees, where they ordinarily dwell, they hurl themselves at any animal that passes by, and with greater speed than an arrow they impale it — the venomous serpent dying in the act, as does the wounded animal, which likewise perishes.

entities_mentioned.name: amatinca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of pit viper (Bothrops sp.) or another arboreal viper native to the Andean montane forests; the 'needle on the forehead' may refer to a rostral or supraocular scale projection. The leaping/self-impaling behavior is folkloric and not biologically attested.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of the snake launching itself from trees and dying upon striking its prey is a widespread early modern myth (cf. similar accounts of the 'flying serpent' in European and colonial literature). No known snake species behaves in this manner or dies upon biting. The 'needle on the forehead' is an unusual morphological detail that does not correspond clearly to any confirmed Andean species.

entities_mentioned.name: Vilcabamba (province)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vilcabamba region, Cusco Department, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR rendered as 'Bilcabamba'; corrected to 'Vilcabamba', the well-known Andean province in the diocese of Cuzco.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'amatinca' as 'ainatin-m a' (split across a line break) and 'Vilcabamba' as 'Bilcabamba'; both have been silently corrected as the intended words are unambiguous. The word 'serpiente' was also split by OCR as 'ser-pwentc'; corrected to 'serpiente'. No medical or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment; it is a natural-history/zoological description. The claim that the snake dies upon striking its prey is a characteristic early modern folk belief and should not be taken as accurate natural history.

results.title_inferred: The Quirquinchu (Armadillo): Description, Habits, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO L
Del quirquinchu

El quirquinchu es un animal de extraña y maravillosa hechura, del tamaño de un puerco de un año y muy parecido a él en el hocico y talle; de cortas piernas y larga cola; en lugar del pellejo tiene todo el cuerpo armado de fuertes conchas a modo de corazas, en esta forma: que toda la espalda e ijares es una concha recia, no lisa por la parte convexa, sino con unas partes relevadas sobre otras por lo ancho, que parecen cintas del anchor de un dedo, que lo ciñen; En las demás partes del cuerpo tiene sus coyunturas, para poder usar de sus miembros, con sólo el vientre desarmado, por donde le suelen sacar toda la carne, dejando entera la armazón. De la concha de las espaldas, guarnecida en oro o plata, se hacen curiosas tazas en que beber; y de la concha de la cola, que es toda una, de partes desiguales, con que tiene la haz escamosa, semejante a la cáscara de la piña y es de dos o tres palmos de largo, suelen usar los indios por trompeta en sus bailes y regocijo.

Hace su madriguera el quirquinchu debajo de tierra, cavando con las uñas, en las cuales tiene tanta fuerza, que cuando se va entrando en su manida, si le echan mano de los pies para sacarlo, se ase con ellas en la tierra tan fuertemente, que da bien que hacer a un hombre hasta que, de cansado, se desase, y así lo sacan a fuerza. Es tan cauteloso y astuto, que en tiempo de algunos aguaceros, antes de entrar la fuerza de las aguas, se echa de espalda, haciendo en su vientre un hoyo, mediante sus conchas, en que recoge el agua que puede caber, y aguarda desta suerte a que los venados sedientos vengan a beber a aquel charquito, porque respecto de ser la tierra donde esto acaece caliente y falta de agua, al punto se consume la de las lluvias, excepto en la fuerza dellas; y al tiempo que el incauto venado mete la boca en el agua, le agarra con las uñas y dientes, de manera que aunque por desasirse da mil brincos, no lo suelta hasta que, rendido el venado y sin aliento, cae en tierra, y entonces el quirquinchu lo desangra y mata; por lo cual los suelen llamar mata venados; aunque también por las conchas de que está cubierto lo llaman armadillo. Hay tres o cuatro suertes de quirquinchus que no se diferencian mucho unos de otros. Su carne es buen manjar, y su unto aprovecha para los nervios encogidos y para resolver tumores duros. Los polvos de los huesecillos de la cola, bebidos con agua tibia y zumo de limón, valen para despedir la piedra de la vejiga y riñones y para detención de orina. Con los polvos de sus conchas suelen sahumar útilmente a las criaturas. Llámase quirquinchu en la lengua peruana.
results.translation:
CHAPTER L
On the Quirquinchu

The quirquinchu is an animal of strange and wondrous form, the size of a one-year-old pig and very similar to one in its snout and build; with short legs and a long tail. Instead of a hide, its entire body is armored with hard shells [conchas] in the manner of a coat of armor, in the following fashion: the whole of the back and flanks forms a stiff shell, not smooth on the convex side, but with parts raised above others across the width, resembling bands [cintas] about a finger's breadth wide that encircle it. On the other parts of the body it has joints so that it can use its limbs, with only the belly unarmored, through which the flesh is commonly removed, leaving the shell-frame intact. From the shell of the back, mounted in gold or silver, curious drinking cups are made; and from the shell of the tail—which is all one piece, of unequal sections, giving it a scaly surface resembling the rind of a pineapple, and two or three hand-spans [palmos] in length—the Indians commonly use it as a trumpet in their dances and festivities.

The quirquinchu makes its burrow underground, digging with its claws, in which it has such great strength that when it is entering its den, if one grabs it by the feet to pull it out, it grips the earth with its claws so firmly that it gives a man considerable trouble until, exhausted, it lets go, and in this way it is extracted by force. It is so cautious and cunning that during certain rainstorms, before the full force of the waters arrives, it lies on its back, forming a hollow in its belly between its shells in which it collects as much water as will fit, and waits in this manner for thirsty deer to come and drink from that little pool—for the land where this occurs is hot and lacking in water, and the rainwater disappears at once except during the height of the downpour. When the unwitting deer dips its mouth into the water, the quirquinchu seizes it with claws and teeth in such a way that, though the deer leaps a thousand times in its attempt to break free, the quirquinchu does not release it until, overcome and breathless, the deer falls to the ground, whereupon the quirquinchu bleeds it and kills it. For this reason they are commonly called deer-killers [mata venados]; though they are also called armadillo on account of the shells with which they are covered. There are three or four kinds of quirquinchos that do not differ greatly from one another. Their flesh is good eating, and their fat [unto] is beneficial for contracted [shrunken] nerves and for resolving hard tumors. The powder of the small bones of the tail, drunk with warm water and lemon juice, is efficacious for expelling kidney and bladder stones and for retention of urine. The powder of their shells is commonly used with good effect as a fumigation [sahumerio] for infants. It is called quirquinchu in the Peruvian tongue [i.e., Quechua].

entities_mentioned.name: quirquinchu / armadillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Armadillo spp. (family Dasypodidae); likely Chaetophractus vellerosus or a related species common to Andean/South American regions
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes three or four varieties that do not differ greatly; the Quechua name quirquinchu and the Spanish gloss armadillo are both recorded. Medical uses attributed: fat (unto) for contracted nerves and hard tumors; powdered tail bones (huesecillos de la cola) in warm water with lemon juice for urinary stones and urinary retention; powdered shells used as fumigation for infants.

entities_mentioned.name: unto del quirquinchu (armadillo fat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preparation: applied topically or internally (method not specified) for nervios encogidos (contracted/shrunken nerves) and tumores duros (hard tumors).

entities_mentioned.name: polvos de los huesecillos de la cola (powder of the tail's small bones)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preparation: ground to powder, drunk with warm water and lemon juice (agua tibia y zumo de limón). Indicated for kidney and bladder stones (piedra de la vejiga y riñones) and retention of urine (detención de orina).

entities_mentioned.name: polvos de las conchas (powder of the shells)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preparation: burned or ground to powder and used as a fumigation (sahumerio) for infants (criaturas). Condition treated not specified beyond general benefit to infants.

results.translator_notes: The account of the quirquinchu hunting deer by lying on its back and collecting rainwater is folkloric rather than factual natural history; Cobo is transmitting indigenous oral tradition. The OCR produced several errors: 'tamaño' split as 'tama-^'; 'suelen' rendered as 'sxielen'; 'espalda' as 'esjjal-da'; 'buenos' (in 'buen manjar') rendered as 'Imen'; 'huesecillos' as 'Imesecillos'; 'criaturas' split with spurious spaces; and 'mata venados' garbled with a '>' character. All have been silently corrected as unambiguous OCR errors. The final sentence is incomplete in the source (ends with a comma after 'peruana'), suggesting the segment was cut; no text appears to be missing from the chapter itself as this is likely the chapter's end.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO LI
De los monos

En todas las tierras calientes y de montaña destas Indias se crían gran muchedumbre de monos, de los cuales se hallan tanta diferencia como de perros dentro de su especie; porque los hay de todos tamaños, desde tan pequeños como ratones, hasta del grandor de un crecido galgo. Todos son de una misma hechura, muy parecidos a las monas, salvo que tienen largas colas con que se asen y cuelgan de los árboles. Difieren unos de otros en el tamaño y color, y en tener unos la cara larga y otros redonda; los hay negros, pardos, rubios, bermejos y amarillos. Unos se hallan no mayores que ardillas, que por tener la cabeza y cara semejante a la del león, los llamamos leoncillos, y son muy preciados. Los menores, que son como ratoncillos, son muy de ver y de gran estima; tienen tan pequeña y delgada la voz, que parece canto de algún pajarillo, y es menester guardarlos de los gatos. A los mayores de todos llaman los indios deste reino sacharunas que quiere decir «hombres salvajes y monteses», y les atribuyen particular instinto; porque dicen lo tienen para armar lazos a venados y cazarlos, para su sustento, y que no hablan por no pagar tributo ni hacer otro servicio alguno; con que dan a entender que no les falta más que hablar para aparecer del todo racionales. Son éstos tan grandes, que iguala su estatura a la de un muchacho de doce años; tienen el rostro más a semejanza del humano que otro ninguno; su voz imita a la humana; casi siempre andan en dos pies; mantiénense de frutas silvestres y de alguna carne que hallan.

En las selvas y bosques cerrados, donde ordinariamente hay gran copia de monos, andan siempre sobre los árboles, saltando de unos en otros, sin bajar al suelo, y dan tan grandes voces, que se oyen a gran distancia, y hasta en los navíos que están surtos en los puertos de tierras montuosas, como yo los he oído. Traídos los micos a tierras frías, son muy delicados, y se mueren luego, si no se tiene gran cuidado con ellos. Estando yo en la ciudad del Cuzco, el año de 1609, vi un mico, que lo habían sacado de la provincia de los Andes, tan extraño, que no he visto jamás otro como él. Era del tamaño de un conejo, y en lo que consistía su extrañeza era en que tenía barba y bigotes tan bien hechos como un hombre; y así, como cosa única y rara, se vendió en cien pesos, y lo compró un caballero llamado don Juan Palomino, pero muriósele dentro de breves días.

Crían los micos con grande amor a sus hijos, trayéndolos en brazos a tiempos muy apretadamente; socórrense unos a otros, porque cuando ven a uno herido, le aplican a la herida hojas de árboles, para detener la sangre. Suelen tirar a la gente que pasa por los caminos ramas de árboles, y muéstranse algunos tan entendidos y hacen cosas que causa admiración. A los micos mansos suelen traer atados a un perro, para que se calienten con él; y vi yo uno en la ciudad de Guamanga, que hacía cosas prodigiosas; entre otras, tenía cuidado de dar de comer a su perro, y tirando la cuerda con que estaba amarrado a él, lo llevaba adonde estaban comiendo los criados de casa, y poniéndose junto a ellos, muy disimulado, cuando vía la suya, metía la mano en el plato, y sacando una tajada de carne, la arrojaba lejos, y al punto subiéndose sobre su perro, lo espoleaba; el cual corría con el mico encima y cogía la carne antes que se la pudiesen quitar. Es cosa graciosa ver las monerías y gestos que hace un mico destos que están amarrados a perros, cuando los mismos perros pelean unos con otros. Los indios del Perú llaman al mico, cusillu, y los mexicanos, ozumetli.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LI
On Monkeys

In all the warm and mountainous lands of these Indies there are bred great multitudes of monkeys [monos], among which there is as much variety as there is among dogs within their species; for they come in all sizes, from as small as mice to as large as a full-grown greyhound. All are of the same general form [hechura], very resembling the females of the species [monas], save that they have long tails with which they grasp and hang from the trees. They differ from one another in size and color, and in that some have long faces and others round ones; there are black, grey [pardos], fair [rubios], reddish [bermejos], and yellow ones. Some are found no larger than squirrels, and because their head and face resemble that of a lion, we call them leoncillos [little lions], and they are greatly prized. The smallest, which are like little mice, are very wonderful to behold and held in great esteem; their voice is so small and thin that it seems like the song of some little bird, and one must take care to keep them away from cats. The largest of all are called by the Indians of this kingdom sacharunas, which means 'wild men of the woods,' and they attribute to them a particular instinct; for they say these monkeys have the ability to set snares for deer and hunt them for their sustenance, and that they do not speak so as to avoid paying tribute or rendering any other service — thereby implying that nothing is lacking in them but speech to appear wholly rational. These are so large that their stature equals that of a boy of twelve years; their face resembles the human countenance more than any other creature; their voice imitates the human voice; they nearly always walk on two feet; and they sustain themselves on wild fruits and whatever meat they can find. (22)

In the dense jungles and forests, where ordinarily there is a great abundance of monkeys, they move always through the treetops, leaping from one tree to another without coming down to the ground, and they cry out so loudly that they can be heard from a great distance — even from ships lying at anchor in the ports of mountainous lands, as I myself have heard them. When monkeys [micos] are brought to cold lands, they are very delicate and die quickly if great care is not taken with them. While I was in the city of Cuzco in the year 1609, I saw a monkey that had been brought out of the province of the Andes, so remarkable that I have never seen another like it. It was the size of a rabbit, and what made it so remarkable was that it had a beard and whiskers as well-formed as those of a man; and thus, as something unique and rare, it was sold for one hundred pesos, and was purchased by a gentleman named don Juan Palomino, but it died within a few days. (23)

Monkeys raise their young with great love, carrying them in their arms and holding them very tightly at times; they aid one another, for when they see one of their number wounded, they apply leaves from trees to the wound in order to stop the bleeding. They are wont to throw branches at people passing along the roads, and some show themselves so intelligent and perform such things as to cause wonder. Tame monkeys [micos] are commonly kept tied to a dog so that they may warm themselves against it; and I myself saw one in the city of Guamanga [modern Ayacucho] that did prodigious things. Among other feats, it made a habit of feeding its dog, and by pulling the rope by which it was tied to the animal, it would lead the dog to where the household servants were eating; and placing itself among them very slyly, when it saw its opportunity, it would thrust its hand into the dish, and pulling out a piece of meat, would fling it far away, whereupon it would instantly climb up on its dog and spur it on; the dog would run with the monkey on its back and seize the meat before anyone could take it away. It is an amusing thing to see the monkey-tricks [monerías] and faces made by one of these monkeys tied to a dog when the dogs themselves are fighting one another. The Indians of Peru call the monkey cusillu, and the Mexicans call it ozumetli.

entities_mentioned.name: monos / micos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: New World monkeys (Order Primates, various genera)
entities_mentioned.notes: General term covering a wide range of New World monkey species.

entities_mentioned.name: leoncillos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pygmy marmoset (Cebuella pygmaea) or lion tamarins (Leontopithecus spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for lion-like facial mane; squirrel-sized. Most likely the pygmy marmoset or a tamarin species.

entities_mentioned.name: sacharunas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a large spider monkey or woolly monkey (Ateles spp. or Lagothrix spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term meaning 'wild/forest men.' Cobo's footnote (22) suggests the genus Brachyurus [sic, likely Brachyteles or Cacajao]. Bipedal tendencies and large size suggest Lagothrix or Ateles.

entities_mentioned.name: Pithecia / Chiropotes (beard-and-whisker monkey, Cuzco, 1609)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pithecia spp. or Chiropotes spp. (saki or bearded saki monkey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's footnote (23) explicitly suggests a species of Pithecia or Chiropotes based on the distinctive beard and whiskers described.

entities_mentioned.name: cusillu
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name for monkey; used by the Indians of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: ozumetli
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl name for monkey, as given by Cobo. Likely the Nahuatl 'ozomatli,' the standard term for monkey.

results.translator_notes: This chapter contains no medical or pharmacological content; it is a natural history and ethnographic account of New World monkeys. Cobo's two numbered footnotes (22 and 23) appear in the original and have been preserved in the translation. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'muchedumbre de monos^ dé' → 'muchedumbre de monos, de'; 'uni' → 'una'; 'ardulas' → 'ardillas'; 'qiie' → 'que'; 'tienyr' (mid-word line break artifact) → 'tiempos'; 'adon-dí»' → 'adonde'; 'mctm' → 'metía'; 'cana-ñas' → 'caminos'; 'ffuitar' → 'quitar'; 'otsumetli» ' → 'ozumetli'; 'Brfr-chiurus' (footnote 22) → 'Brachyurus' (or Brachyteles). The city of Guamanga is the colonial name for present-day Ayacucho, Peru. 'Monerías' is a period Spanish term meaning monkey-tricks or buffoonery, preserved in context.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
Del zahino

El zahino es cierta casta de puerco distinto de los de Europa; de pequeño cuerpo, no mayor que un gran lechón, algo bermejo y de pocas carnes. Tiene en mitad del lomo un lobanillo que parece el ombligo, donde se recoge un humor tan hediondo, que si en matando el animal no se lo quitan, lo inficiona y corrompe muy en breve. Dicen algunos que el zahino tiene el ombligo en las espaldas, pensando que lo es el dicho lobanillo; mas no es así, sino que tiene su ombligo donde los demás animales. Andan estos puercos en manadas con gran ruido, y algunas son tan grandes, que van mil juntos con sus lechones. Reconocen y siguen su capitán con tanta constancia, que jamás lo desamparan aunque les cueste la vida. Suélenlos cazar para comer y su carne es de buen gusto.

Tiene cosas prodigiosas este animal; porque es animoso y feroz como un león; de ningún animal huye, a cualquiera espera y hace rostro, y lo mismo a la gente; antes, en viendo un hombre, arremete a él con gran furia. Tiene gran odio y enemistad con el tigre, porque el tigre gusta mucho de su carne; el cual, para cazarlos, se pone escondido por donde los zahinos han de pasar, y esto es de ordinario junto a un árbol, para tener donde guarecerse; y cuando ellos van pasando, acomete al postrero y de una manotada lo abre; mas, apenas lo ha herido y el puerquecillo levanta la voz, cuando revuelven todos los de la manada sobre el tigre, el cual se libra de sus enemigos subiéndose ligeramente en el árbol, y los zahinos, con gran rabia, suelen estar más de dos horas dando crueles navajadas en el tronco, hasta que, cansándose, se van. Entonces baja el tigre y se come la caza que dejó muerta. A veces acaece coger los zahinos al tigre en parte que no se les puede huir, adonde es para ver la batalla tan cruel y sangrienta que se traba; porque lo cercan por todas partes haciendo un cerrado y apiñado escuadrón, y se van para él afilando sus navajas; el tigre, que es animal ferocísimo, se embravece sobre manera, y jugando de sus garras, ejecuta su fiereza haciendo en los puercos gran matanza; pero ellos pelean tan porfiadamente, que aunque cuesta las vidas de muchos, no desisten de su empresa hasta dejarlo muerto y despedazado al tigre los que dellos quedan vivos.

Suélense cazar estos puercos deste modo: pónese el que va a caza dellos junto a un árbol, y da una voz donde ellos están, o hace algún ruido para que lo oigan, que no es menester más para que al punto acudan; y antes que lleguen, se sube el cazador con presteza en el árbol, al cual embisten ellos con gran ímpetu y lo comienzan a morder; en esta sazón los va el cazador desde el árbol alanceando y matando, con advertencia de no herir al capitán (que mientras éste no muriese o se fuere, todos morirán y no huirá ninguno). En habiendo muerto los que quiere, hiere o mata al capitán, el cual, herido, huye y tras él todos los demás; o si muere, también se van todos. Ha menester el que anda en esta caza ponerse con tiempo en cobro, porque si los puercos lo cogen, los despedazarán, como lo hicieron a un negro en Tierra Firme, que no subió al árbol con tanta presteza que no le pudiesen asir; al cual despedazaron en un instante y se lo comieron sin dejar más que los huesos. En las provincias de Tierra Firme se dice a este animal zahina, y en este reino del Perú lo llaman sus naturales guangana.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LV
On the Peccary [zahino]

The peccary [zahino] is a particular breed of pig distinct from those of Europe; small in body, no larger than a great suckling pig, somewhat reddish and with little flesh. It has in the middle of its back a cyst [lobanillo] that resembles a navel, in which gathers a humor so foul-smelling that if it is not removed immediately upon killing the animal, it infects and corrupts the meat very quickly. Some people say that the peccary has its navel on its back, believing the said cyst to be such; but this is not so, for it has its navel in the same place as other animals. These pigs travel in herds with great noise, and some herds are so large that a thousand go together with their young. They recognize and follow their leader with such steadfastness that they never abandon him even at the cost of their lives. They are commonly hunted for food and their meat is of good flavor.

This animal has remarkable qualities; for it is spirited and fierce as a lion; it flees from no animal, stands its ground and faces down any creature, and does the same with people—indeed, upon seeing a man, it charges at him with great fury. It harbors great hatred and enmity toward the jaguar [tigre], because the jaguar is very fond of its flesh; the jaguar, in order to hunt them, hides along the path where the peccaries are to pass, and this is ordinarily beside a tree, so as to have a place of refuge; and as they pass by, it attacks the last one and with a single swipe tears it open; but scarcely has it wounded the little pig and the animal raises its cry, than the entire herd turns back upon the jaguar, which saves itself from its enemies by nimbly climbing the tree, while the peccaries, in great fury, are accustomed to spend more than two hours delivering savage slashes [navajadas] at the trunk, until, growing tired, they depart. Then the jaguar climbs down and eats the prey it left dead. Sometimes it happens that the peccaries catch the jaguar in a place from which it cannot escape, and there one can witness the cruel and bloody battle that ensues; for they surround it on all sides forming a close and compact squadron, and advance upon it honing their tusks [navajas]; the jaguar, which is a most ferocious animal, flies into extraordinary rage and, plying its claws, unleashes its ferocity making great slaughter among the pigs; yet they fight so stubbornly that, though it costs the lives of many, they do not abandon their enterprise until those remaining alive leave the jaguar dead and torn to pieces.

These pigs are commonly hunted in this manner: the hunter positions himself beside a tree and calls out toward where they are, or makes some noise so they can hear him—nothing more is needed for them to come at once; and before they arrive, the hunter swiftly climbs the tree, which they assail with great force and begin to bite; at this point the hunter, from the tree, proceeds to lance and kill them, taking care not to wound the leader (for so long as the leader does not die or depart, all will remain and none will flee). Having killed as many as he wishes, he then wounds or kills the leader, who, upon being wounded, flees and all the others follow; or if the leader dies, they all depart as well. One engaged in this hunt must take care to get to safety in good time, because if the pigs catch him they will tear him to pieces, as they did to a Black man [negro] in Tierra Firme who did not climb the tree quickly enough to prevent them from seizing him; they tore him to pieces in an instant and ate him, leaving nothing but his bones. In the provinces of Tierra Firme this animal is called zahina, and in this kingdom of Peru the native inhabitants call it guangana.

entities_mentioned.name: zahino / zahina / guangana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Collared peccary (Pecari tajacu) or white-lipped peccary (Tayassu pecari); given the description of very large herds of up to a thousand and extreme ferocity, the white-lipped peccary is more likely
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes the dorsal scent gland (described as a 'lobanillo' or cyst), which is a defining anatomical feature of peccaries. The name 'guangana' is recorded as the Peruvian indigenous term; 'zahino/zahina' is used in Tierra Firme (present-day Panama/Colombia). The scent gland must be removed immediately after slaughter to prevent tainting the meat, a practice still followed today.

entities_mentioned.name: tigre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaguar (Panthera onca)
entities_mentioned.notes: In colonial Spanish American usage, 'tigre' invariably refers to the jaguar, not the Old World tiger.

results.translator_notes: This chapter contains no materia medica content; it is a natural history / zoological description of the peccary. The dorsal musk gland is accurately described. The Spanish 'navajas' (literally 'razors' or 'knives') is used metaphorically for the peccary's sharp tusks. The word 'constancia' in the original was printed 'eonstanca' (clear OCR error, silently corrected). 'Negro' is translated literally as 'Black man' following scholarly convention for period documents. The 'tigre' as jaguar note is flagged for archive users unfamiliar with colonial Spanish zoological terminology.
